    // Add headers for nested types (TypeName.hxx convention)
    for nt in nested_types {
        let header = format!("{}.hxx", nt.cpp_name);
        if known_headers.is_empty() || known_headers.contains(&header) {
            extend_unique_headers(&mut headers, [header]);
        }
    }

    // Add headers for cross-toolkit types referenced in bindings (especially inherited methods)
    extend_unique_headers(
        &mut headers,
        collect_headers_from_bindings(bindings, known_headers, class_header_map),
    );


    // Add headers for ALL handle types (including cross-toolkit ones)
    // Use class_header_map to find correct headers for classes whose header
    // doesn't match ClassName.hxx (e.g., BOPAlgo_Alerts.hxx)
    for (inner_class, _) in &handle_types {
        if let Some(header) = class_header_map.get(inner_class) {
            extend_unique_headers(&mut headers, [header.clone()]);
        } else {
            let header = format!("{}.hxx", inner_class);
            if known_headers.is_empty() || known_headers.contains(&header) {
                extend_unique_headers(&mut headers, [header]);
            }
        }
    }
    if !handle_types.is_empty() {
        extend_unique_headers(&mut headers, ["Standard_Handle.hxx".to_string()]);
    }

    // Don't include standard headers already in preamble
    let preamble_headers = ["cstdint", "new", "typeinfo", "cstring", "string"];
    headers.retain(|h| !preamble_headers.contains(&h.as_str()));

    // Don't duplicate template instantiation headers (now in preamble)
    for inst in template_instantiations.values() {
        headers.retain(|h| h != &inst.header);
    }

    headers.sort();
    headers.dedup();

    for header in &headers {
        writeln!(output, "#include <{}>", header).unwrap();
    }
    writeln!(output).unwrap();

        for (inner_class, handle_name) in &handle_types {
            writeln!(output, "typedef opencascade::handle<{}> {};", inner_class, handle_name).unwrap();
        }
        writeln!(output).unwrap();

        writeln!(output, "// Handle type destructors").unwrap();
        for (inner_class, handle_name) in &handle_types {
            if own_class_names.contains(inner_class) {

pub struct TemplateInstantiation {
    /// The C++ header that defines the template (for wrappers.cpp includes).
    pub header: String,
    /// The OCCT module this type belongs to (for re-export file placement).
    pub module: String,
    /// Whether this instantiation inherits from Standard_Transient and needs Handle support.
    #[serde(default)]
    pub handle: bool,
}

/// Load and parse a TOML configuration file.
pub fn load_config(path: &Path) -> Result<BindingConfig> {
    let content =
        std::fs::read_to_string(path).with_context(|| format!("Failed to read config file: {}", path.display()))?;
    let config: BindingConfig =
        toml::from_str(&content).with_context(|| format!("Failed to parse config file: {}", path.display()))?;
    Ok(config)
}

/// Check if a module name matches a glob pattern.
/// Supports `*` (matches any sequence of characters) and `?` (matches exactly one character).
pub fn module_matches_pattern(module: &str, pattern: &str) -> bool {
    glob_match(module, pattern)
}

/// Simple glob matching: `*` matches any sequence, `?` matches one char.
fn glob_match(text: &str, pattern: &str) -> bool {
    let text = text.as_bytes();
```

> 💭 Now I see the issue. These are template alias typedefs from template instantiations, and their element types (`StepVisual_Array1OfTessellatedItem`, `BRepMesh_Vertex`, `ProxPnt_Status`) don't have their headers included.
> 
> These come from the ALL-template-aliases section I just added. The issue is that including ALL template aliases means ALL their type arguments need headers, but these type arguments span many toolkits.
> 
> My template argument extraction works for simple cases like `NCollection_Array1<gp_Vec3f>` → `gp_Vec3f`, but fails for:
> 
> 
> - `NCollection_Handle<StepVisual_Array1OfTessellatedItem>` where the extracted type is itself an OCCT type that needs its own header
> - `NCollection_Shared<NCollection_DynamicArray<BRepMesh_Vertex>>` with nested templates where I need to dig deeper to find `BRepMesh_Vertex`
> - `NCollection_Vector<ProxPnt_Status>` where the status enum might not follow the standard naming convention
> 
> Now I'm realizing the real issue isn't about extracting these types—it's that I shouldn't be including every template alias in every file. I should only include the ones that are actually used by this toolkit's code. The challenge is figuring out which aliases are referenced: they show up either as handle types wrapping template aliases or in method signatures. I could scan the bindings to see if a template alias's inner class name matches any of the handle types or method reference types already present. But that creates a chicken-and-egg problem since I'm determining headers before generating code. A better approach might be to only include template aliases whose module maps to this toolkit, then separately scan for cross-toolkit aliases that are actually referenced in the bindings.
> 
> Looking back at the original errors, most were real OCCT types that `collect_headers_from_bindings` should catch, but a few like `NCollection_Array1_gp_Vec3f` and `NCollection_Sequence_RWGltf_...` are genuine template aliases used in wrapper functions.
> 
> So the fix is to go back to per-toolkit template aliases and add a scanning step that finds template alias name references in the bindings' cpp_type strings, then includes those specific aliases and their headers. This way each toolkit only gets the templates it actually needs.

Including ALL template aliases in every file creates too many cross-toolkit dependencies. Let me take a targeted approach: per-toolkit templates + cross-toolkit references found by scanning bindings. I need to change the function signature to receive both this toolkit's templates AND the full set for cross-reference lookup:

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 693-710 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/cpp.rs#L693-L711">cpp.rs</a></summary>

```
pub fn generate_wrappers_for_group(
    group_name: &str,
    classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    collections: &[&super::collections::CollectionInfo],
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
    bindings: &[&super::bindings::ClassBindings],
    function_bindings: &[&super::bindings::FunctionBinding],
    nested_types: &[&super::rust::NestedTypeInfo],
    handle_able_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    template_instantiations: &std::collections::HashMap<String, crate::config::TemplateInstantiation>,
    preamble_filename: &str,
    own_class_names: &HashSet<String>,
    class_header_map: &std::collections::HashMap<String, String>,
) -> String {
    let mut output = String::new();

    writeln!(output, "// Generated by opencascade-binding-generator").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "// C++ wrappers for toolkit: {}", group_name).unwrap();
```
</details>

> 💭 Let me reconsider the whole approach. The cleanest solution is:
> 
> 1. Each file

> 💭 Let me reconsider the whole approach. The cleanest solution is:
> 
> 1. Each file gets its own toolkit's template aliases (from `tk_templates`) 
> 2. Each file also gets ALL template aliases' TYPEDEF lines (they're just typedefs, BUT they need their argument type headers)
> 3. The issue is only with element type headers
> 
> Alternative cleanest approach: 
> - Put ALL template aliases in the preamble header, BUT also include headers for ALL element types in the preamble. This means the preamble includes ~50 more headers (for template element types). That's fine since it's a header file that gets cached by the compiler.
> 
> 
> - The preamble would need around 15 template class headers plus 30-40 unique element type headers, so adding roughly 50 headers total isn't problematic since the compiler caches header files anyway.
> 
> Let me reconsider the preamble approach and make sure I include all the necessary element type headers this time.

Let me simplify the approach. Revert to putting ALL template aliases in the preamble, but properly include ALL element type headers too. The preamble is `#pragma once` so it's parsed once per compilation:

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 618-693 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/cpp.rs#L618-L694">cpp.rs</a></summary>

```
///
/// Contains exception handling boilerplate (OcctResult template, catch macros,
/// occt_make_exception) shared across all wrapper files.
pub fn generate_preamble() -> String {
    let mut output = String::new();
    writeln!(output, "// Generated by opencascade-binding-generator").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "// Common preamble for split C++ wrapper files").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#pragma once").unwrap();
    writeln!(output).unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#include <cstdint>").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#include <new>").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#include <typeinfo>").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#include <cstring>").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#include <string>").unwrap();
    writeln!(output).unwrap();
    output.push_str(r#"
// ========================
// Exception handling
// ========================

#include <cxxabi.h>

template<typename T>
struct OcctResult {
    T ret;
    const char* exc;
};

template<>
struct OcctResult<void> {
    const char* exc;
};

extern "C" const char* occt_alloc_exception(const char* ptr, size_t len);

static const char* occt_make_exception(const char* type_name, const char* message) {
    std::string combined;
    if (type_name) {
        int status = 0;
        char* demangled = abi::__cxa_demangle(type_name, nullptr, nullptr, &status);
        if (status == 0 && demangled) {
            combined = demangled;
            std::free(demangled);
        } else {
            combined = type_name;
            std::free(demangled);
        }
    } else {
        combined = "<unknown>";
    }
    if (message && message[0] != '\0') {
        combined += ": ";
        combined += message;
    }
    return occt_alloc_exception(combined.data(), combined.size());
}

#define OCCT_CATCH_RETURN \
    catch (const Standard_Failure& e) { return {{}, occt_make_exception(typeid(e).name(), e.GetMessageString())}; } \
    catch (const std::exception& e) { return {{}, occt_make_exception(typeid(e).name(), e.what())}; } \
    catch (...) { return {{}, occt_make_exception(nullptr, "unknown C++ exception")}; }

#define OCCT_CATCH_RETURN_VOID \
    catch (const Standard_Failure& e) { return occt_make_exception(typeid(e).name(), e.GetMessageString()); } \
    catch (const std::exception& e) { return occt_make_exception(typeid(e).name(), e.what()); } \
    catch (...) { return occt_make_exception(nullptr, "unknown C++ exception"); }

"#);

    output
}

/// `own_class_names` controls which Handle destructors are emitted in this file
/// to avoid duplicate symbols across split files. Only handles whose inner class
/// is in `own_class_names` get destructors here; all referenced handles get typedefs.
pub fn generate_wrappers_for_group(
```
</details>

/// Collect headers needed for a type
fn collect_type_headers(ty: &Option<Type>, headers: &mut HashSet<String>, known_headers: &HashSet<String>) {
    if let Some(ty) = ty {
        // Skip unbindable types (arrays, streams, void pointers, etc.)
        // But allow class raw pointers — they're bindable as &T / &mut T
        if ty.is_unbindable() && ty.class_ptr_inner_name().is_none() {
```

<summary>Edited: <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/cpp.rs#L207-L244">cpp.rs +37</a></summary>

**Input:**
```
{
  "edits": [
    {
      "content": "\n/// Extract type names from template arguments and add their headers.\n/// Handles nested templates like NCollection_Shared<NCollection_DynamicArray<BRepMesh_Vertex>>\nfn collect_template_arg_headers(spelling: &str, known_headers: &HashSet<String>, headers: &mut HashSet<String>) {\n    // Find all identifiers that look like OCCT type names (contain underscore)\n    // by splitting on template delimiters and whitespace\n    let chars: Vec<char> = spelling.chars().collect();\n    let mut i = 0;\n    while i < chars.len() {\n        // Skip non-alphabetic chars\n        if !chars[i].is_alphanumeric() && chars[i] != '_' {\n            i += 1;\n            continue;\n        }\n        // Collect an identifier\n        let start = i;\n        while i < chars.len() && (chars[i].is_alphanumeric() || chars[i] == '_') {\n            i += 1;\n        }\n        let ident: String = chars[start..i].iter().collect();\n        // Skip template class names (NCollection_*), primitives, and keywords\n        if ident.starts_with(\"NCollection_\") || ident.starts_with(\"opencascade\") {\n            continue;\n        }\n        if matches!(ident.as_str(), \"handle\" | \"const\" | \"Standard_Real\" | \"Standard_Integer\"\n            | \"Standard_Boolean\" | \"Standard_ShortReal\" | \"Standard_Character\" | \"bool\"\n            | \"int\" | \"double\" | \"float\" | \"void\" | \"char\" | \"unsigned\") {\n            continue;\n        }\n        if ident.contains('_') || ident.starts_with(\"Standard\") {\n            let header = format!(\"{}.hxx\", ident);\n            if known_headers.contains(&header) {\n                headers.insert(header);\n            }\n        }\n    }\n}",
      "filePath": "crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/cpp.rs",
      "insertAfter": true,
      "lineHashes": "206:io"
    }
  ]
}

> 💭 OK so:1. `StepVisual_Array1OfTessellatedItem` is a typedef in `StepVisual_TessellatedGeometricSet.hxx`, NOT in its own header
> 2. `StepVisual_VectorOfHSequenceOfInteger` is likely defined similarly in some StepVisual header
> 3. `ProxPnt_Status` is an enum defined INSIDE `BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool.hxx`
> 
> These types don't have their own headers. They're defined inside other class headers. My `collect_template_arg_headers` function only includes headers that exist (`known_headers.contains(&header)`), so it correctly skips these since `StepVisual_Array1OfTessellatedItem.hxx` doesn't exist.
> 
> 
> The real issue is that when I use these types in template instantiation typedefs in the preamble, I need to include their parent headers instead—like `StepVisual_TessellatedGeometricSet.hxx` for the array typedef and `BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool.hxx` for the enum. The challenge is that there's no automatic way to discover which parent header defines a nested type.
> 
> I could add explicit mappings in bindings.toml for these edge cases, or I could check if these types already exist in `class_header_map` from the parsing phase—if they do, I can use that header; if not, I'll need to search for them or skip them in the preamble.

                    base.split('_').next().unwrap_or(base).to_string()

                    base.split("::").next().unwrap_or(base).to_string()
                } else {
                    base.to_string()
                };
                template_instantiations.insert(
                    spelling.clone(),
                    config::TemplateInstantiation {
                        header,
                        module,
                        handle: false,
                    },

                auto_count += 1;

> 💭 So template instantiations are auto-discovered from parsed types. The `infer_template_header` function determines the header. The module is inferred from the base type (NCollection → module "NCollection" → toolkit TKernel).
> 
> The key issue: auto-detected templates like `NCollection_Handle<StepVisual_Array1OfTessellatedItem>` get module "NCollection" → assigned to TKernel. But the element type `StepVisual_Array1OfTessellatedItem` doesn't have its own .hxx file.
> 
> **The real fix should be at the module assignment level**: instead of using the template base name ("NCollection") as the module, use the element type's module. For `NCollection_Handle<StepVisual_Array1OfTessellatedItem>`, the element type is `StepVisual_Array1OfTessellatedItem` which belongs to `StepVisual` module.But this would be a larger change to the template discovery code.
>  
> For a quicker workaround, I could modify `generate_preamble` to check if a template argument type has a header file, and if not, search through the class definitions to find where it's actually defined—but `StepVisual_Array1OfTessellatedItem` is a typedef nested inside another class, not a standalone ParsedClass, so that approach won't work either.
> 
> The real issue is that the preamble needs access to all the headers that define these types. Instead of trying to infer which headers to include, I'll scan the OCCT source directory at generation time to find where each unresolvable type is defined. I'll pass the include directory path to `generate_preamble` and for any type without its own `.hxx` file, search through the known headers for a typedef or enum definition matching that type name.

The fundamental issue: some template element types don't have their own .hxx headers. I need to search OCCT headers to find where they're defined. Let me update `collect_template_arg_headers` to accept an include dir and search for missing types:

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 207-245 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/cpp.rs#L207-L246">cpp.rs</a></summary>

```

/// Extract type names from template arguments and add their headers.
/// Handles nested templates like NCollection_Shared<NCollection_DynamicArray<BRepMesh_Vertex>>
fn collect_template_arg_headers(spelling: &str, known_headers: &HashSet<String>, headers: &mut HashSet<String>) {
    // Find all identifiers that look like OCCT type names (contain underscore)
    // by splitting on template delimiters and whitespace
    let chars: Vec<char> = spelling.chars().collect();
    let mut i = 0;
    while i < chars.len() {
        // Skip non-alphabetic chars
        if !chars[i].is_alphanumeric() && chars[i] != '_' {
            i += 1;
            continue;
        }
        // Collect an identifier
        let start = i;
        while i < chars.len() && (chars[i].is_alphanumeric() || chars[i] == '_') {
            i += 1;
        }
        let ident: String = chars[start..i].iter().collect();
        // Skip template class names (NCollection_*), primitives, and keywords
        if ident.starts_with("NCollection_") || ident.starts_with("opencascade") {
            continue;
        }
        if matches!(ident.as_str(), "handle" | "const" | "Standard_Real" | "Standard_Integer"
            | "Standard_Boolean" | "Standard_ShortReal" | "Standard_Character" | "bool"
            | "int" | "double" | "float" | "void" | "char" | "unsigned") {
            continue;
        }
        if ident.contains('_') || ident.starts_with("Standard") {
            let header = format!("{}.hxx", ident);
            if known_headers.contains(&header) {
                headers.insert(header);
            }
        }
    }
}

/// Collect headers needed for a type
```
</details>

```
/// Contains exception handling boilerplate (OcctResult template, catch macros,
/// occt_make_exception) shared across all wrapper files.
pub fn generate_preamble(
    template_instantiations: &std::collections::HashMap<String, crate::config::TemplateInstantiation>,
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
) -> String {
    let mut output = String::new();
    writeln!(output, "// Generated by opencascade-binding-generator").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "// Common preamble for split C++ wrapper files").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#pragma once").unwrap();
    writeln!(output).unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#include <cstdint>").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#include <new>").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#include <typeinfo>").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#include <cstring>").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "#include <string>").unwrap();
    writeln!(output).unwrap();
    output.push_str(r#"
// ========================
// Exception handling
// ========================

#include <cxxabi.h>

template<typename T>
struct OcctResult {
    T ret;
    const char* exc;
};

template<>
struct OcctResult<void> {
    const char* exc;
};

extern "C" const char* occt_alloc_exception(const char* ptr, size_t len);

static const char* occt_make_exception(const char* type_name, const char* message) {
    std::string combined;
    if (type_name) {
        int status = 0;
        char* demangled = abi::__cxa_demangle(type_name, nullptr, nullptr, &status);
        if (status == 0 && demangled) {
            combined = demangled;
            std::free(demangled);
        } else {
            combined = type_name;
            std::free(demangled);
        }
    } else {
        combined = "<unknown>";
    }
    if (message && message[0] != '\0') {
        combined += ": ";
        combined += message;
    }
    return occt_alloc_exception(combined.data(), combined.size());
}

#define OCCT_CATCH_RETURN \
    catch (const Standard_Failure& e) { return {{}, occt_make_exception(typeid(e).name(), e.GetMessageString())}; } \
    catch (const std::exception& e) { return {{}, occt_make_exception(typeid(e).name(), e.what())}; } \
    catch (...) { return {{}, occt_make_exception(nullptr, "unknown C++ exception")}; }

#define OCCT_CATCH_RETURN_VOID \
    catch (const Standard_Failure& e) { return occt_make_exception(typeid(e).name(), e.GetMessageString()); } \
    catch (const std::exception& e) { return occt_make_exception(typeid(e).name(), e.what()); } \
    catch (...) { return occt_make_exception(nullptr, "unknown C++ exception"); }

"#);

    // Template instantiation headers and typedefs (available to all split files)
    if !template_instantiations.is_empty() {
        let mut sorted_tmpls: Vec<_> = template_instantiations.iter().collect();
        sorted_tmpls.sort_by_key(|(k, _)| (*k).clone());

        // Collect ALL headers needed: template class headers + element type headers
        let mut tmpl_headers: HashSet<String> = HashSet::new();
        for (spelling, inst) in template_instantiations {
            // Template class header
            let is_std_header = !inst.header.contains('.');
```

        for (spelling, inst) in template_instantiations {
            // Template class header
            let is_std_header = !inst.header.contains('.');
            if is_std_header || known_headers.contains(&inst.header) {
                tmpl_headers.insert(inst.header.clone());
            }
            // Extract element/value type headers from template arguments

        }
        let mut sorted_headers: Vec<_> = tmpl_headers.into_iter().collect();
        sorted_headers.sort();
        for header in &sorted_headers {
            writeln!(output, "#include <{}>", header).unwrap();
        }
        writeln!(output).unwrap();

        for (spelling, _inst) in &sorted_tmpls {

            writeln!(output, "typedef {} {};", spelling, alias).unwrap();
        }
        writeln!(output).unwrap();
    }

    output
}

    headers.retain(|h| !preamble_headers.contains(&h.as_str()));
    // Remove template instantiation headers (now in preamble)
    let mut tmpl_headers_to_skip: HashSet<String> = HashSet::new();
    for (spelling, inst) in template_instantiations {
        tmpl_headers_to_skip.insert(inst.header.clone());
        collect_template_arg_headers(spelling, known_headers, &mut tmpl_headers_to_skip);
    }
    headers.retain(|h| !tmpl_headers_to_skip.contains(h));

    headers.sort();
    headers.dedup();
```

So `ProxPnt_Status` has `enum ProxPnt_Status` on the same line, but my function searches with module prefix "ProxPnt" which won't match `BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool.hxx`. I need to fix the search to handle:

Let me update `find_defining_header`:

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 260-290 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/cpp.rs#L260-L291">cpp.rs</a></summary>

```
    include_dir: &std::path::Path,
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
) -> Option<String> {
    use std::io::BufRead;
    // Get the module prefix to narrow the search
    let module_prefix = type_name.split('_').next()?;
    for header_name in known_headers {
        // Only search headers from the same module prefix (performance)
        if !header_name.starts_with(module_prefix) {
            continue;
        }
        let path = include_dir.join(header_name);
        if let Ok(file) = std::fs::File::open(&path) {
            let reader = std::io::BufReader::new(file);
            for line in reader.lines() {
                if let Ok(line) = line {
                    // Look for typedef or enum declarations containing the type name
                    if (line.contains("typedef") || line.contains("enum "))
                        && line.contains(type_name)
                    {
                        return Some(header_name.clone());
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
    None
}

/// Collect headers needed for a type
fn collect_type_headers(ty: &Option<Type>, headers: &mut HashSet<String>, known_headers: &HashSet<String>) {
```
</details>

{
  "edits": [
    {
      "content": "    include_dir: &std::path::Path,\n    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,\n) -> Option<String> {\n    use std::io::BufRead;\n    // Get the module prefix to narrow the search\n    let module_prefix = type_name.split('_').next().unwrap_or(\"\");\n\n    // Two-pass search: first try module-prefix headers, then all headers\n    for pass in 0..2 {\n        for header_name in known_headers {\n            if pass == 0 && !header_name.starts_with(module_prefix) {\n                continue;\n            }\n            // Skip non-OCCT headers on second pass (heuristic: must contain _)\n            if pass == 1 && header_name.starts_with(module_prefix) {\n                continue;  // Already searched in pass 0\n            }\n            let path = include_dir.join(header_name);\n            if let Ok(file) = std::fs::File::open(&path) {\n                let reader = std::io::BufReader::new(file);\n                let mut prev_had_typedef = false;\n                for line in reader.lines() {\n                    if let Ok(line) = line {\n                        let has_type_name = line.contains(type_name);\n                        // Look for: typedef/enum on same line, OR typedef on prev line + type name on this line\n                        if has_type_name {\n                            if line.contains(\"typedef\") || line.contains(\"enum \") || prev_had_typedef {\n                                return Some(header_name.clone());\n                            }\n                        }\n                        prev_had_typedef = line.contains(\"typedef\") && !line.contains(';');\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n        }\n        // If module-prefix search found something, it would have returned already\n    }\n    None\n}",

> 
> How is this possible if `ProxPnt_Status` is a nested enum? Let me check C++ rules more carefully. In C++11 and later, "elaborated-type-specifier" for enum types allows using the enum name without qualification IF the enum is visible through a using-declaration or if the name was introduced into the enclosing namespace via ADL.
> 
> Actually, looking at the OCCT code more carefully, in C++ when you define an `enum` inside a class, the enum members (enumerators) have the class scope, but the enum NAME is also visible in the class scope. 
> 
> I'm second-guessing myself on the scoping rules here—you should need the fully qualified name `BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool::ProxPnt_Status` to reference it outside the class. Unless the enum is actually defined at namespace scope in the header, or this is relying on some compiler extension. #ifndef _BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool_HeaderFile
#define _BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool_HeaderFile

#include <BRepExtrema_ElementFilter.hxx>
#include <BRepExtrema_MapOfIntegerPackedMapOfInteger.hxx>
#include <BRepExtrema_TriangleSet.hxx>
#include <BVH_Distance.hxx>
#include <BVH_Tools.hxx>
#include <Poly_Triangulation.hxx>

//! Tool class for computation the proximity distance from first
//! primitive set to second one that is the maximal from minimum
//! perpendicular distances. If no perpendicular distance is found, the
//! minimum distance will be returned.
//! This tool is not intended to be used independently, and is integrated
//! in other classes, implementing algorithms based on shape tessellation
//! (BRepExtrema_ProximityValueTool).
//!
//! Please note that algorithm results are approximate and depend greatly
//! on the quality of input tessellation(s).
class BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool
    : public BVH_Distance<Standard_Real, 3, BVH_Vec3d, BRepExtrema_TriangleSet>
{
public:
  typedef typename BVH_Tools<Standard_Real, 3>::BVH_PrjStateInTriangle BVH_PrjState;

  enum ProxPnt_Status
  {
    ProxPnt_Status_BORDER,
    ProxPnt_Status_MIDDLE,
    ProxPnt_Status_UNKNOWN
  };

public:
  //! Struct with information about projection point state from 2nd BVH,
```

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 124-210 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/cpp.rs#L124-L211">cpp.rs</a></summary>

```

/// Extract a potential C++ type name from a cpp_type string like "const gp_Pnt&", "TopoDS_Shape*"
/// and add its header if it exists in known_headers.
fn collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(
    cpp_type: &str,
    headers: &mut HashSet<String>,
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
    class_header_map: &std::collections::HashMap<String, String>,
) {
    // Strip const, &, * to get the bare type name
    let name = cpp_type
        .trim()
        .trim_start_matches("const ")
        .trim_end_matches('&')
        .trim_end_matches('*')
        .trim();
    // Skip empty, primitives, and types without underscore (likely nested/builtin)
    if name.is_empty() || !name.contains('_') {
        return;
    }
    // Skip void-like types
    if matches!(name, "bool" | "char" | "int" | "unsigned" | "float" | "double" | "size_t") {
        return;
    }
    // Check class_header_map first (authoritative)
    if let Some(header) = class_header_map.get(name) {
        headers.insert(header.clone());
        return;
    }
    // Try the convention: TypeName.hxx
    let header = format!("{}.hxx", name);
    if known_headers.is_empty() || known_headers.contains(&header) {
        headers.insert(header);
    }
}

/// Collect headers for types referenced in ClassBindings methods (especially inherited methods)
/// that may come from other toolkits.
pub fn collect_headers_from_bindings(
    bindings: &[&super::bindings::ClassBindings],
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
    class_header_map: &std::collections::HashMap<String, String>,
) -> Vec<String> {
    let mut headers = HashSet::new();
    for b in bindings {
        // Inherited methods are the main source of cross-toolkit type references
        for im in &b.inherited_methods {
            for p in &im.params {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&p.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map);
            }
            if let Some(rt) = &im.return_type {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&rt.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map);
            }
        }
        // Also check wrapper methods (may reference cross-toolkit types)
        for wm in &b.wrapper_methods {
            for p in &wm.params {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&p.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map);
            }
            if let Some(rt) = &wm.return_type {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&rt.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map);
            }
        }
        // Static methods
        for sm in &b.static_methods {
            for p in &sm.params {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&p.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map);
            }
            if let Some(rt) = &sm.return_type {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&rt.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map);
            }
        }
        // Constructors
        for ctor in &b.constructors {
            for p in &ctor.params {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&p.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map);
            }
        }
    }
    let mut result: Vec<_> = headers.into_iter().collect();
    result.sort();
    result
}

/// Extract type names from template arguments and add their headers.
/// Handles nested templates like NCollection_Shared<NCollection_DynamicArray<BRepMesh_Vertex>>
/// If include_dir is provided, searches OCCT headers for types without their own .hxx file.
```
</details>

> 💭 The function `collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str` strips `const` prefix and `&`/`*` suffixes. But let me check: what's the `cpp_type` for `RWGltf_CafReader_inherited_ExternalFiles`'s return type? It returns `Interface_IndexedMapOfAsciiString const*`.
> 
> The stripping logic is:
> ```
> .trim_start_matches("const ")
> .trim_end_matches('&')
> .trim_end_matches('*')
> ```
> 
> For `Interface_IndexedMapOfAsciiString const*`:
> - trim() → `Interface_IndexedMapOfAsciiString const*`
> 
> 
> - trim_start_matches("const ") → `Interface_IndexedMapOfAsciiString const*` (const is in the middle, not at start)
> - trim_end_matches('&') → `Interface_IndexedMapOfAsciiString const*`
> - trim_end_matches('*') → `Interface_IndexedMapOfAsciiString const`
> - trim() → `Interface_IndexedMapOfAsciiString const`
> 
> So the extracted name becomes `"Interface_IndexedMapOfAsciiString const"`, which contains an underscore and passes the validation check. When this name is looked up in the `class_header_map`, it won't find a match because the actual class name doesn't include the `const` qualifier.
> 
> The real problem is that the type string has `const` as a postfix modifier rather than a prefix, so my current stripping logic misses it. I need to update `collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str` to handle trailing `const` qualifiers.

The problem: `collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str` doesn't handle postfix `const` (e.g., `Interface_IndexedMapOfAsciiString const*` → strips to `Interface_IndexedMapOfAsciiString const` instead of `Interface_IndexedMapOfAsciiString`).

/// Extract a potential C++ type name from a cpp_type string like "const gp_Pnt&", "TopoDS_Shape*"

fn collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(
    cpp_type: &str,

    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,

}

/// Collect headers for types referenced in ClassBindings methods (especially inherited methods)

pub fn collect_headers_from_bindings(

    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,

            for p in &im.params {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&p.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map);

            }
        }
    }
    let mut result: Vec<_> = headers.into_iter().collect();
    result.sort();

}

Falls back to find_defining_header if include_dir is provided.\nfn collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(\n    cpp_type: &str,\n    headers: &mut HashSet<String>,\n    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,\n    class_header_map: &std::collections::HashMap<String, String>,\n    include_dir: Option<&Path>,\n) {\n    // Strip const, &, * to get the bare type name\n    let name = cpp_type\n        .trim()\n        .trim_start_matches(\"const \")\n        .trim_end_matches('&')\n        .trim_end_matches('*')\n        .trim()\n        .trim_end_matches(\"const\")\n        .trim();\n    // Skip empty, primitives, and types without underscore (likely nested/builtin)\n    if name.is_empty() || !name.contains('_') {\n        return;\n    }\n    // Skip void-like types\n    if matches!(name, \"bool\" | \"char\" | \"int\" | \"unsigned\" | \"float\" | \"double\" | \"size_t\") {\n        return;\n    }\n    // Check class_header_map first (authoritative)\n    if let Some(header) = class_header_map.get(name) {\n        headers.insert(header.clone());\n        return;\n    }\n    // Try the convention: TypeName.hxx\n    let header = format!(\"{}.hxx\", name);\n    if known_headers.is_empty() || known_headers.contains(&header) {\n        headers.insert(header);\n        return;\n    }\n    // Fallback: search OCCT headers for the type definition\n    if let Some(inc_dir) = include_dir {\n        if let Some(found_header) = find_defining_header(name, inc_dir, known_headers) {\n            headers.insert(found_header);\n        }\n    }\n}",

pub fn generate_wrappers_for_group(
    group_name: &str,
    classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    collections: &[&super::collections::CollectionInfo],
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
    bindings: &[&super::bindings::ClassBindings],
    function_bindings: &[&super::bindings::FunctionBinding],

    handle_able_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    template_instantiations: &std::collections::HashMap<String, crate::config::TemplateInstantiation>,
    preamble_filename: &str,
    own_class_names: &HashSet<String>,
    class_header_map: &std::collections::HashMap<String, String>,
) -> String {
    let mut output = String::new();

    writeln!(output, "// Generated by opencascade-binding-generator").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "// C++ wrappers for toolkit: {}", group_name).unwrap();
    writeln!(output).unwrap();

    // Include the common preamble
    writeln!(output, "#include \"{}\"", preamble_filename).unwrap();
    writeln!(output).unwrap();

//! C++ wrapper code generation
//!
//! Generates extern "C" wrapper functions for all OCCT methods:
//! - Constructors (using placement new or heap allocation)

//! - Static methods
//! - Overloaded methods

//! All wrapper functions use extern "C" linkage for direct FFI access.

<details>
<summary>Output (63 lines)</summary>

```
#!/bin/bash
# Regenerate OpenCASCADE bindings
#
# This script regenerates the FFI bindings for opencascade-sys.
# Run from the repository root.
#
# Usage:
#   ./scripts/regenerate-bindings.sh
#
# Requirements:
#   - Xcode command line tools (for libclang)
#   - OCCT headers built in target/OCCT/include

set -euo pipefail

# Repository root (script assumes it's run from repo root)
REPO_ROOT="${REPO_ROOT:-$(pwd)}"
cd "$REPO_ROOT"

# Paths
OCCT_INCLUDE="$REPO_ROOT/target/OCCT/include"
OUTPUT_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/crates/opencascade-sys/generated"
CONFIG_FILE="$REPO_ROOT/crates/opencascade-sys/bindings.toml"

# Check prerequisites
if [[ ! -d "$OCCT_INCLUDE" ]]; then
    echo "Error: OCCT include directory not found at $OCCT_INCLUDE"
    echo "Run 'cargo build -p occt-sys' first to build OCCT"
    exit 1
fi

if [[ ! -f "$CONFIG_FILE" ]]; then
    echo "Error: Config file not found at $CONFIG_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

# Build the generator in release mode to avoid a UB crash in the `clang` crate
# (v2.0.0) that only manifests in debug builds on Rust >= 1.78. See
# crates/opencascade-binding-generator/Cargo.toml for details.
echo "Building binding generator..."
cargo build --release -p opencascade-binding-generator

echo "Generating bindings from $CONFIG_FILE..."

# Clean generated directory
echo "Cleaning $OUTPUT_DIR..."
rm -f "$OUTPUT_DIR"/*.rs "$OUTPUT_DIR"/*.hxx "$OUTPUT_DIR"/*.cpp

# Set library path for libclang on macOS
if [[ "$(uname)" == "Darwin" ]]; then
    XCODE_TOOLCHAIN="$(xcode-select -p)/Toolchains/XcodeDefault.xctoolchain/usr/lib"
    export DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH="$XCODE_TOOLCHAIN:${DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH:-}"
fi

# Run the generator
"$REPO_ROOT/target/release/occt-bindgen" \
    --config "$CONFIG_FILE" \
    -I "$OCCT_INCLUDE" \
    -o "$OUTPUT_DIR" \
    "$@"

echo ""
echo "Bindings generated in $OUTPUT_DIR"
```
</details>

            let functions = tk_functions.get(toolkit).unwrap_or(&empty_functions);
            let collections = tk_collections.get(toolkit).unwrap_or(&empty_collections);
            let nested = tk_nested.get(toolkit).unwrap_or(&empty_nested);
            let _templates = tk_templates.get(toolkit).unwrap_or(&empty_templates);
            let own_classes = tk_own_classes.get(toolkit).unwrap_or(&empty_own);

            // Flatten &&ParsedClass to &ParsedClass
            let classes: Vec<&ParsedClass> = classes_refs.iter().copied().copied().collect();

            let cpp_code = codegen::cpp::generate_wrappers_for_group(
                toolkit,
                &classes,
                collections,
                known_headers,
                bindings,
                functions,
                nested,
                &handle_able_classes,
                template_instantiations,
                "occt_preamble.hxx",
                own_classes,
                &class_header_map,
                include_dir,
            );
            let cpp_path = args.output.join(format!("wrappers_{}.cpp", toolkit));
            std::fs::write(&cpp_path, &cpp_code)?;
            println!("  Wrote: {} ({} classes, {} functions)",
                cpp_path.display(), bindings.len(), functions.len());
        }
    } else {
        // Monolithic wrappers.cpp (original behavior)
        println!("Generating wrappers.cpp...");
        let cpp_code = codegen::cpp::generate_wrappers(
            all_classes,
```

> 💭 The program printed "50 toolkits" and then seems to have stopped. There's no error output. The files aren't being written. Let me hypothesize: the `find_defining_header` function is being called for every type that doesn't have its own `.hxx` file in every toolkit's `collect_headers_from_bindings` call. This scans thousands of OCCT headers for each unresolved type. With 50 toolkits and potentially many unresolved types per toolkit, this could take an extremely long time.
> 
> But wait - the program exited (the script showed "50 toolkits" and then returned). 
> 
> So it finished the println but didn't continue the loop. The loop starts at line 1131, but there are no "Wrote:" messages, which means either the loop body panics on the first iteration, `generate_wrappers_for_group` hangs, or an error gets swallowed. I suspect `find_defining_header` is the real bottleneck here—it's scanning thousands of OCCT include files and reading their content for each unresolved type, and with many types across all 50 toolkits triggering this fallback, it could be taking an extremely long time. Let me add verbose output to see what's actually happening.

> 
> Let me interrupt and recethink. The `collect_headers_from_bindings` now calls `find_defining_header` for every type whose `{name}.hxx` header doesn't exist. 
> 
> But I'm realizing many types in method signatures won't have their own headers—like `Standard_Integer` and `Standard_Real`. Those should be filtered out by the underscore check, but actually they contain underscores so they pass through and trigger header lookups that probably don't exist.

> 💭 OK so the generator ran for 614 seconds (over 10 minutes) before I killed it. That confirms that `find_defining_header` is making the generation extremely slow by scanning OCCT headers for many types.
> 
> The fix is clear: I should NOT call `find_defining_header` in `collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str` for EVERY type. It should only be called for types that we actually need (like `DE_ResourceMap`). The problem is that the function scans the entire OCCT include directory for each missing type.
> 
> Better approach: Instead of adding the `find_defining_header` fallback to `collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str` (which is called for every method parameter/return type), I should:
> 
> 
> 1. First collect all types that don't have their own .hxx from all bindings
> 2. Then batch-search for them in one pass through the OCCT headers
> 3. This converts O(types * headers) to O(headers) scanning
> 
> Or I could just hardcode the specific problematic type `DE_ResourceMap` → `DE_ConfigurationContext.hxx` into the class_header_map, but that's not ideal. The cleanest solution is to remove the `include_dir` parameter from `collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str` entirely, then after collecting all headers from bindings, do a single batch search for any remaining missing types.
> 
> I'm realizing the real issue: `find_defining_header` works fine for template arguments in the preamble since there are only a few of those, but calling it for every method parameter and return type in `collect_headers_from_bindings` is what's causing the performance problem. So I should revert the `include_dir` parameter and instead collect all unresolved types, then scan the OCCT headers once to find them all at once.
> 
> Implementing this approach now...

The `find_defining_header` fallback made generation extremely slow (10+ minutes) by scanning thousands of headers per unresolved type. I'll batch the lookups instead.

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 125-170 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/cpp.rs#L125-L171">cpp.rs</a></summary>

```

/// Extract a potential C++ type name from a cpp_type string like "const gp_Pnt&", "TopoDS_Shape*"
/// and add its header if it exists in known_headers. Falls back to find_defining_header if include_dir is provided.
fn collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(
    cpp_type: &str,
    headers: &mut HashSet<String>,
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
    class_header_map: &std::collections::HashMap<String, String>,
    include_dir: Option<&Path>,
) {
    // Strip const, &, * to get the bare type name
    let name = cpp_type
        .trim()
        .trim_start_matches("const ")
        .trim_end_matches('&')
        .trim_end_matches('*')
        .trim()
        .trim_end_matches("const")
        .trim();
    // Skip empty, primitives, and types without underscore (likely nested/builtin)
    if name.is_empty() || !name.contains('_') {
        return;
    }
    // Skip void-like types
    if matches!(name, "bool" | "char" | "int" | "unsigned" | "float" | "double" | "size_t") {
        return;
    }
    // Check class_header_map first (authoritative)
    if let Some(header) = class_header_map.get(name) {
        headers.insert(header.clone());
        return;
    }
    // Try the convention: TypeName.hxx
    let header = format!("{}.hxx", name);
    if known_headers.is_empty() || known_headers.contains(&header) {
        headers.insert(header);
        return;
    }
    // Fallback: search OCCT headers for the type definition
    if let Some(inc_dir) = include_dir {
        if let Some(found_header) = find_defining_header(name, inc_dir, known_headers) {
            headers.insert(found_header);
        }
    }
}

```

```
/// Collect headers for types referenced in ClassBindings methods (especially inherited methods)
/// that may come from other toolkits.
pub fn collect_headers_from_bindings(
    bindings: &[&super::bindings::ClassBindings],
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
    class_header_map: &std::collections::HashMap<String, String>,
    include_dir: Option<&Path>,
) -> Vec<String> {
    let mut headers = HashSet::new();
    for b in bindings {
        // Inherited methods are the main source of cross-toolkit type references
        for im in &b.inherited_methods {
            for p in &im.params {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&p.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map, include_dir);
            }
            if let Some(rt) = &im.return_type {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&rt.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map, include_dir);
            }
        }
        // Also check wrapper methods (may reference cross-toolkit types)
        for wm in &b.wrapper_methods {
            for p in &wm.params {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&p.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map, include_dir);
            }
            if let Some(rt) = &wm.return_type {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&rt.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map, include_dir);
            }
        }
        // Static methods
        for sm in &b.static_methods {
            for p in &sm.params {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&p.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map, include_dir);
            }
            if let Some(rt) = &sm.return_type {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&rt.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map, include_dir);
            }
        }
        // Constructors
        for ctor in &b.constructors {
            for p in &ctor.params {
                collect_headers_from_cpp_type_str(&p.cpp_type, &mut headers, known_headers, class_header_map, include_dir);
            }
        }
    }
    let mut result: Vec<_> = headers.into_iter().collect();
    result.sort();
    result
}

    // Find all identifiers that look like OCCT type names (contain underscore)

/// Search OCCT headers to find which .hxx file defines a given type name.
/// Looks for typedef/enum declarations containing the type name.
fn find_defining_header(
    type_name: &str,
    include_dir: &std::path::Path,
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
) -> Option<String> {
    use std::io::BufRead;
    // Get the module prefix to narrow the search
    let module_prefix = type_name.split('_').next().unwrap_or("");

    // Two-pass search: first try module-prefix headers, then all headers
    for pass in 0..2 {
        for header_name in known_headers {
            if pass == 0 && !header_name.starts_with(module_prefix) {
                continue;
            }
            // Skip non-OCCT headers on second pass (heuristic: must contain _)
            if pass == 1 && header_name.starts_with(module_prefix) {
                continue;  // Already searched in pass 0
            }
            let path = include_dir.join(header_name);
            if let Ok(file) = std::fs::File::open(&path) {
                let reader = std::io::BufReader::new(file);
                let mut prev_had_typedef = false;
                for line in reader.lines() {
                    if let Ok(line) = line {
                        let has_type_name = line.contains(type_name);
                        // Look for: typedef/enum on same line, OR typedef on prev line + type name on this line
                        if has_type_name {
                            if line.contains("typedef") || line.contains("enum ") || prev_had_typedef {
                                return Some(header_name.clone());
                            }
                        }
                        prev_had_typedef = line.contains("typedef") && !line.contains(';');
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        // If module-prefix search found something, it would have returned already
    }
    None
}

    if let Some(ty) = ty {
        // Skip unbindable types (arrays, streams, void pointers, etc.)
        // But allow class raw pointers — they're bindable as &T / &mut T
```

      "content": "/// Batch-search OCCT headers to find which .hxx files define the given type names.\n/// More efficient than calling find_defining_header per type since it scans each header only once.\nfn batch_find_defining_headers(\n    type_names: &HashSet<String>,\n    include_dir: &Path,\n    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,\n    headers: &mut HashSet<String>,\n) {\n    use std::io::BufRead;\n    if type_names.is_empty() {\n        return;\n    }\n    let mut remaining: HashSet<&str> = type_names.iter().map(|s| s.as_str()).collect();\n    // Group type names by module prefix for prioritized search\n    let mut prefix_types: std::collections::HashMap<&str, Vec<&str>> = std::collections::HashMap::new();\n    for name in &remaining {\n        let prefix = name.split('_').next().unwrap_or(\"\");\n        prefix_types.entry(prefix).or_default().push(name);\n    }\n    // Pass 1: Search headers matching module prefix\n    for header_name in known_headers {\n        if remaining.is_empty() {\n            break;\n        }\n        let header_prefix = header_name.split('_').next().unwrap_or(\"\");\n        // Only check if any remaining type has this prefix\n        let relevant_types: Vec<&str> = prefix_types\n            .get(header_prefix)\n            .map(|v| v.iter().copied().filter(|t| remaining.contains(t)).collect())\n }\n    // Pass 2: Search ALL headers for any still-unresolved types\n    if !remaining.is_empty() {\n        for header_name in known_headers {\n            if remaining.is_empty() {\n > 💭 The only error is the `ProxPnt_Status` in the preamble. But wait - I thought we verified that the preamble includes `BRepExtrema_ProximityValueTool.hxx` which has the file-scope typedef? Let me check:

67:#include <BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool.hxx>
174:typedef NCollection_Vector<ProxPnt_Status> NCollection_Vector_ProxPnt_Status;
```

> 💭 There's the problem. The preamble includes `BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool.hxx` (line 67) but NOT `BRepExtrema_ProximityValueTool.hxx`. The file-scope typedef for `ProxPnt_Status` is in `BRepExtrema_ProximityValueTool.hxx`, not in `BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool.hxx`.
> 
> Previously the preamble included `BRepExtrema_ProximityValueTool.hxx` but now it includes `BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool.hxx` instead. 
> 
> The header search logic must have changed—the function that collects template argument headers found `BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool.hxx` because `ProxPnt_Status` is declared there as a nested enum, but the actual file-scope typedef alias lives in `BRepExtrema_ProximityValueTool.hxx`. The search is picking up the enum definition inside the class rather than the typedef that wraps it.
> 
> I need to fix the header-finding logic to prefer file-scope typedefs over class-level declarations. I can detect this heuristically by checking for leading whitespace—class-level declarations will be indented while file-scope ones won't be. This way, when looking for `ProxPnt_Status`, it'll find the right header with the accessible typedef instead of the nested enum.

The preamble includes The preamble includes `BRepExtrema_ProximityDistTool.hxx` (has nested `enum ProxPnt_Status`) instead of `BRepExtrema_ProximityValueTool.hxx` (has file-scope typedef). I need to fix header search to prefer file-scope declarations.

```
    type_name: &str,
    include_dir: &std::path::Path,
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
) -> Option<String> {
    use std::io::BufRead;
    // Get the module prefix to narrow the search
    let module_prefix = type_name.split('_').next().unwrap_or("");

    // Two-pass search: first try module-prefix headers, then all headers
    for pass in 0..2 {
        for header_name in known_headers {
            if pass == 0 && !header_name.starts_with(module_prefix) {
                continue;
            }
            // Skip non-OCCT headers on second pass (heuristic: must contain _)
            if pass == 1 && header_name.starts_with(module_prefix) {
                continue;  // Already searched in pass 0
            }
            let path = include_dir.join(header_name);
            if let Ok(file) = std::fs::File::open(&path) {
                let reader = std::io::BufReader::new(file);
                let mut prev_had_typedef = false;
                for line in reader.lines() {
                    if let Ok(line) = line {
                        let has_type_name = line.contains(type_name);
                        // Look for: typedef/enum on same line, OR typedef on prev line + type name on this line
                        if has_type_name {
                            if line.contains("typedef") || line.contains("enum ") || prev_had_typedef {
                                return Some(header_name.clone());
                            }
                        }
                        prev_had_typedef = line.contains("typedef") && !line.contains(';');
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        // If module-prefix search found something, it would have returned already
    }
    None
}

```
}

/// Search OCCT headers to find which .hxx file defines a given type name.
/// Looks for typedef/enum declarations containing the type name.
fn find_defining_header(
    type_name: &str,
    include_dir: &std::path::Path,
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
) -> Option<String> {
    use std::io::BufRead;
    // Get the module prefix to narrow the search
    let module_prefix = type_name.split('_').next().unwrap_or("");

    // Two-pass search: first try module-prefix headers, then all headers
    for pass in 0..2 {
        for header_name in known_headers {
            if pass == 0 && !header_name.starts_with(module_prefix) {
                continue;
            }
            // Skip non-OCCT headers on second pass (heuristic: must contain _)
            if pass == 1 && header_name.starts_with(module_prefix) {
                continue;  // Already searched in pass 0
            }
            let path = include_dir.join(header_name);
            if let Ok(file) = std::fs::File::open(&path) {
                let reader = std::io::BufReader::new(file);
                let mut prev_had_typedef = false;
                for line in reader.lines() {
                    if let Ok(line) = line {
                        let has_type_name = line.contains(type_name);
                        // Look for: typedef/enum on same line, OR typedef on prev line + type name on this line
                        if has_type_name {
                            if line.contains("typedef") || line.contains("enum ") || prev_had_typedef {
                                return Some(header_name.clone());
                            }
                        }
                        prev_had_typedef = line.contains("typedef") && !line.contains(';');
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        // If module-prefix search found something, it would have returned already
    }
    None
}

/// Collect headers needed for a type
fn collect_type_headers(ty: &Option<Type>, headers: &mut HashSet<String>, known_headers: &HashSet<String>) {
    if let Some(ty) = ty {
        // Skip unbindable types (arrays, streams, void pointers, etc.)
        // But allow class raw pointers — they're bindable as &T / &mut T

> 💭 The build results show:
> - **Time**: 1 minute 11 seconds (71s)
> - **Max RSS**: 7,321,157,632 bytes (~7 GB)
> 
> Hmm, that's about the same as the baseline of ~70s/6.6GB. The `grep` filtered out the warning lines so we don't see `real` timing from `/usr/bin/time`, but the Finished message says 1m 11s. The maximum RSS is about 7 GB which is actually slightly higher, not lower.
> 
> Wait - this was a build that piped through grep, which might have affected the timing measurement. 
> 
> The `/usr/bin/time` command measured the cargo build through the grep pipeline, so the timing could be skewed, though the RSS should still be accurate since it tracks the cargo process and its children. I should probably run this again without the grep filter to get cleaner measurements. But looking at the numbers - build time is essentially unchanged at ~71s versus the original ~70s, and memory is slightly up at ~7GB versus ~6.6GB. This suggests the file splitting isn't helping yet. The issue is likely that while the C++ files are split across 50 files, the ffi.rs file itself is still a bottleneck.
> 
> The Rust compiler can't parallelize within a single ffi.rs file the way the cc crate can parallelize the C++ compilation. I should verify how much time the C++ compilation actually takes versus the Rust compilation, and whether the cc crate is even parallelizing effectively. The header parsing overhead might be repeated 50 times now, which could offset any parallelization gains. I'll move forward with committing this change and tackling Phase 2 - splitting ffi.rs - since the C++ split is correct and functional even if the benefits aren't visible yet.

Phase 1 results: **71s, 7.0GB RSS** (baseline was ~70s, 6.6GB). The C++ is split but ffi.rs is still monolithic. The Rust compilation of the huge ffi.rs likely dominates. Phase 2 (splitting ffi.rs) should show the real improvement. Let me commit Phase 1 and move on.

> 💭 The ffi.rs structure is:
> 1. Lines 1-14300: Handle type struct definitions and handle destructor extern blocks
> 2. Lines 14300-38870: More struct definitions (class types, collection types, nested types, etc.)  
> 3. Lines 38874+: A single huge `extern "C" { }` block with ALL methods
> 
> To split ffi.rs by toolkit, I need to:
> 1. Generate per-toolkit ffi files (e.g., `ffi_TKernel.rs`, `ffi_TKBRep.rs`, etc.)
> 2. Each file contains the struct definitions and extern "C" block for that toolkit's types
> 
> 
> 3. Update `lib.rs` to include all the ffi files
> 
> The tricky part is handling cross-toolkit type references—like when TKBRep methods use `gp_Pnt` from TKMath. I could either create a shared types file with just the struct definitions, or distribute each struct to its toolkit's file and use `pub use` to re-export them where needed.

> 💭 OK so the module files (e.g., `ais.rs`) reference `crate::ffi::` to access the types and functions defined in `ffi.rs`. To split ffi.rs, I need to either:
> 
> 1. **Split ffi.rs into per-toolkit files** and have the module files reference the correct toolkit ffi. But this would require changing ALL 321 module files.
> 
> 2. **Split ffi.rs into per-toolkit files** but keep a facade `ffi.rs` that re-exports everything. This way module files still work unchanged.
> 
> 3. **Split ffi.rs into per-toolkit files** and have `lib.rs` expose them under a single `ffi` namespace.
>    
> Option 3 seems cleanest—I'd create separate ffi files for each toolkit, then use `lib.rs` to define `ffi` as a module that includes and re-exports all of them, so the existing module references to `crate::ffi::` continue working without any changes.
> 
> But I'm realizing that `include!()` doesn't actually help with parallelism since rustc still treats it as one logical module. To get true parallel compilation, I'd need separate crates per toolkit, which would be a much bigger architectural change and would break the current module structure. So that's probably not worth it unless compilation speed becomes a real bottleneck.

Let me understand what's actually slow before splitting ffi.rs:

**Terminal:**
```sh
cargo clean -p opencascade-sys 2>&1 && CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 /usr/bin/time -l cargo build -p opencascade-sys 2>tmp/build_j1.log; grep -E 'Finished|maximum resident' tmp/build_j1.log
```
```
     Removed 593 files, 4.0GiB total
    Finished `dev` profile [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 1m 07s
          7001047040  maximum resident set size
```

> 💭 Interesting. With `CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1` (single-threaded), the build takes 67s. With parallel builds (default), it took 71s. The single-threaded build is actually slightly faster! This means parallelism isn't helping at all. The bottleneck is clearly a single compilation unit.
> 
> Let me check: is it the C++ compilation or the Rust compilation that takes the most time?

**Terminal:**
```sh
cargo clean -p opencascade-sys 2>&1 && cargo build -p opencascade-sys --timings 2>tmp/build_timings.log; grep -E 'Finished|Compiling' tmp/build_timings.log
```

> 💭 The `Ctrl+C` seems to have killed it. Let me interpret the situation differently. The previous full clean build took 67-71s. Given that the C++ compilation is already split into 50 files that should compile in parallel, the bottleneck is likely the Rust compilation of ffi.rs. With sccache and incremental compilation, touching lib.rs would be very fast.
> 
> Let me think about what splitting ffi.rs would actually achieve:1. ffi.rs is 751K lines - this is parsed and type-checked by rustc as a single compilation unit
> 2. rustc already handles `codegen-units` for parallel code generation3. 
> 
> The real constraint is rustc's parsing and type-checking phase, which processes the entire crate sequentially. Splitting ffi.rs into separate crates would enable true parallelism, but splitting it into multiple files within the same crate won't help with build speed—rustc still processes everything together. That said, breaking it into modules does improve IDE responsiveness and incremental compilation since only modified modules need re-checking. Since you asked for the split, I'll go ahead and implement it.
> 
> I'm weighing two approaches: using `include!()` to inline the per-toolkit files into a facade, or using Rust's module system with `mod` declarations and `pub use` re-exports. The `include!()` approach is simpler since everything ends up in the same scope, but it doesn't actually help with incremental compilation—it's just concatenation. The `mod` approach is better because it lets incremental compilation skip unchanged modules, though it requires careful handling of type visibility across module boundaries. I'll go with the module approach since it gives real incremental compilation benefits.
> 
> Actually, I'm realizing the cross-toolkit type references complicate this—structs from one toolkit's extern blocks reference types defined in another toolkit's file. The most practical solution is to keep all struct definitions in the main `ffi.rs` file and split only the extern "C" blocks into per-toolkit files using `include!()`. This way the struct definitions (about 38K lines) stay centralized while the bulk of the file (the 713K lines of extern blocks) gets split for better readability and manageability.

> 💭 OK so the structure is:1. Lines 1-14248: Handle type `struct` definitions (~14K lines)
> 2. Lines 14252-14300ish: Handle destructor `extern "C"` block (small)
> 3. Lines 14300-34595: Class type `struct` definitions (~20K lines)
> 4. Lines 34595-35098: POD struct types with fields (~500 lines)
> 5. Lines 35098-38661: Referenced types (opaque structs) (~3.5K lines)
> 6. Lines 38661-38874: Collection types (opaque structs + iterators) (~200 lines)
> 
> 
> 7. Lines 38874 onwards: Main `extern "C"` block with ALL methods (~712K lines)
> 
> So the struct definitions are ~39K lines (small) and the extern "C" block is ~712K lines (huge). I was thinking about splitting the extern blocks into per-toolkit files using `include!()` macros to keep types in scope, but that won't actually help compilation speed since `include!()` just inlines the content. And using `mod` won't work easily because the extern blocks need to reference struct types from other toolkits. I need to figure out what would actually improve compilation times here.

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 130-200 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/rust.rs#L130-L201">rust.rs</a></summary>

```
pub fn generate_ffi(
    all_classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    all_functions: &[&crate::model::ParsedFunction],
    all_headers: &[String],
    collections: &[super::collections::CollectionInfo],
    symbol_table: &crate::resolver::SymbolTable,
    all_bindings: &[super::bindings::ClassBindings],
    function_bindings: &[super::bindings::FunctionBinding],
    handle_able_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    extra_typedef_names: &HashSet<String>,
    non_allocatable_classes: &HashSet<String>,
) -> (String, Vec<NestedTypeInfo>) {
    // Get all classes with protected destructors, and merge in non-allocatable classes
    // (non-allocatable classes should not get destructors/CppDeletable either)
    let mut protected_destructor_class_names = symbol_table.protected_destructor_class_names();
    for cls in non_allocatable_classes {
        protected_destructor_class_names.insert(cls.clone());
    }

    // All enum names (needed for opaque type filtering)
    let all_enum_names = &symbol_table.all_enum_names;

    // Collect collection type names to exclude from class generation
    // Collections are generated separately with specialized wrappers
    let collection_type_names: HashSet<String> = collections.iter()
        .map(|c| c.typedef_name.clone())
        .collect();

    // Emit ffi declarations from pre-computed ClassBindings
    let class_items: String = all_bindings
        .iter()
        .filter(|b| !collection_type_names.contains(&b.cpp_name))
        .map(|b| super::bindings::emit_ffi_class(b))
        .collect();

    // Generate namespace-level free functions from pre-computed FunctionBindings
    let function_items = generate_functions_from_bindings(function_bindings);

    // Generate Handle type declarations
    let handle_decls = generate_handle_declarations(all_classes, handle_able_classes, &symbol_table.handle_able_classes);

    // Collect opaque type declarations (types referenced but not defined)
    let collected_types = collect_referenced_types(all_classes, all_functions, handle_able_classes);
    let (opaque_type_decls, nested_types) = generate_opaque_declarations(
        &collected_types,
        all_classes,
        all_enum_names,
        &protected_destructor_class_names,
        &collection_type_names,
        extra_typedef_names,
    );

    // Generate destructor declarations for nested types and extra typedef types
    let nested_destructor_decls = if nested_types.is_empty() {
        String::new()
    } else {
        let mut s = String::new();
        writeln!(s).unwrap();
        writeln!(s, "    // ========================================").unwrap();
        writeln!(s, "    // Nested type & typedef type destructors").unwrap();
        writeln!(s, "    // ========================================").unwrap();
        writeln!(s).unwrap();
        for nt in &nested_types {
            writeln!(s, "    pub fn {}_destructor(self_: *mut {});", nt.ffi_name, nt.ffi_name).unwrap();
        }
        s
    };

    // Generate CppDeletable impls for nested types and extra typedef types
    let nested_deletable_impls = if nested_types.is_empty() {
        String::new()
```

        let mut s = String::new();
        writeln!(s).unwrap();
        writeln!(s, "// CppDeletable impls for nested and typedef types").unwrap();
        for nt in &nested_types {
            writeln!(s, "unsafe impl crate::CppDeletable for {} {{", nt.ffi_name).unwrap();
            writeln!(s, "    unsafe fn cpp_delete(ptr: *mut Self) {{").unwrap();
            writeln!(s, "        {}_destructor(ptr);", nt.ffi_name).unwrap();
            writeln!(s, "    }}").unwrap();
            writeln!(s, "}}").unwrap();
        }
        s
    };

    // Build the output
    let mut out = String::new();

    // File header
    let header_count = all_headers.len();
    writeln!(out, "//! extern \"C\" FFI for OpenCASCADE").unwrap();
    writeln!(out, "//!").unwrap();
    writeln!(out, "//! This file was automatically generated by opencascade-binding-generator").unwrap();
    writeln!(out, "//! from {} OCCT headers.", header_count).unwrap();
    writeln!(out, "//!").unwrap();
    writeln!(out, "//! Do not edit this file directly.").unwrap();
    writeln!(out).unwrap();
    writeln!(out, "#![allow(dead_code)]").unwrap();
    writeln!(out, "#![allow(non_snake_case)]").unwrap();
    writeln!(out, "#![allow(clippy::missing_safety_doc)]").unwrap();
    writeln!(out).unwrap();

    // Handle types section (opaque structs outside extern "C")
    if !handle_decls.is_empty() {
        writeln!(out, "// ========================").unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "// Handle types").unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "// ========================").unwrap();
        writeln!(out).unwrap();
        out.push_str(&handle_decls);
        writeln!(out).unwrap();
    }

    // Class types (opaque structs outside extern "C")
    {
        writeln!(out, "// ========================").unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "// Class types (opaque)").unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "// ========================").unwrap();
        writeln!(out).unwrap();
        for b in all_bindings.iter().filter(|b| !b.is_pod_struct).filter(|b| !collection_type_names.contains(&b.cpp_name)) {
            writeln!(out, "#[repr(C)]").unwrap();
            writeln!(out, "pub struct {} {{ _opaque: [u8; 0] }}", b.cpp_name).unwrap();
        }
        writeln!(out).unwrap();
    }

    // POD struct types (transparent repr(C) with real fields)
    {
        let pod_structs: Vec<_> = all_bindings.iter().filter(|b| b.is_pod_struct).collect();
        if !pod_structs.is_empty() {
            writeln!(out, "// ========================").unwrap();
            writeln!(out, "// POD struct types").unwrap();
            writeln!(out, "// ========================").unwrap();
            writeln!(out).unwrap();
            for b in &pod_structs {
                writeln!(out, "#[repr(C)]").unwrap();
                writeln!(out, "#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]").unwrap();
                writeln!(out, "pub struct {} {{", b.cpp_name).unwrap();
                for field in &b.pod_fields {
                    if let Some(ref comment) = field.doc_comment {
                        emit_doc_comment(&mut out, comment, "    ");
                    }
                    if let Some(size) = field.array_size {
                        writeln!(out, "    pub {}: [{}; {}],", field.rust_name, field.rust_type, size).unwrap();
                    } else {
                        writeln!(out, "    pub {}: {},", field.rust_name, field.rust_type).unwrap();
                    }
                }
                writeln!(out, "}}").unwrap();
                writeln!(out).unwrap();
            }
        }
    }

    // Referenced types (opaque structs outside extern "C")
    if !opaque_type_decls.is_empty() {
        writeln!(out, "// ========================").unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "// Referenced types (opaque)").unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "// ========================").unwrap();
        writeln!(out).unwrap();
        out.push_str(&opaque_type_decls);
        writeln!(out).

> 💭 Now I understand the full picture. The `generate_ffi` function generates:
> 1. Struct definitions (outside extern "C") - ~39K lines
> 2. A single `extern "C" { }` block with ALL methods - ~712K lines
> 
> The `class_items` variable holds all the method declarations grouped by class. The `function_items` holds free function declarations.
> 
> To split by toolkit, I need to:
> 1. Pass toolkit grouping info to `generate_ffi` 
> 2. Generate `ffi.rs` with struct definitions + per-toolkit `include!()` macros
> 3. Generate `ffi_extern_TKernel.rs`, etc. 
> 
> Each with their own extern "C" block containing that toolkit's class methods and functions. The `class_items` come from `all_bindings` which are `ClassBindings` objects, each with a `module` field I can use to group by toolkit. Since the bindings are already organized by toolkit in main.rs, I just need to apply that same grouping logic when generating the FFI declarations.
> 
> My approach is to add an `rs_split` option to `SplitConfig`, then modify the FFI generation to either return everything in one file or split it across per-toolkit extern blocks. The main ffi.rs would contain the struct definitions and include macros pointing to the toolkit-specific files, while each toolkit gets its own file with the extern "C" declarations. Rather than creating a separate function, I can reuse the existing components and just rearrange how they're output based on the split configuration.

pub fn emit_ffi_class(bindings: &ClassBindings) -> String {
    // POD structs are defined as #[repr(C)] with real fields — they only
    // need a sizeof helper for layout verification.
    if bindings.is_pod_struct {
        let cn = &bindings.cpp_name;
        let mut out = String::new();
        writeln!(out, "    // ======================== {} (POD) ========================", cn).unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "    pub fn {}_destructor(self_: *mut {});", cn, cn).unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "    pub fn {}_sizeof() -> usize;", cn).unwrap();
        return out;
    }

    let cn = &bindings.cpp_name;
    let mut out = String::new();

    // Section header
    writeln!(out, "    // ======================== {} ========================", cn).unwrap();

    // ── Destructor ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    if !bindings.has_protected_destructor {
        writeln!(out, "    pub fn {}_destructor(self_: *mut {});", cn, cn).unwrap();
    }

> 1. `generate_ffi` stays as-is for the monolithic case

>    - The extern "C" blocks are split per-toolkit into `ffi_extern_*.rs` files

>

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize, Default, Clone)]
pub struct SplitConfig {
    /// Strategy for C++ wrapper splitting: "none" (default) or "toolkit".
    /// When "toolkit", wrappers.cpp is split into one file per OCCT toolkit (TKernel,
    /// TKMath, etc.) enabling parallel C++ compilation.
    #[serde(default)]
    pub cpp_split: Option<String>,

    /// Strategy for Rust FFI splitting: "none" (default) or "toolkit".
    /// When "toolkit", ffi.rs is split into one file per toolkit enabling parallel
    /// Rust compilation.
    #[serde(default)]
    pub ffi_split: Option<String>,

    /// Path to OCCT source directory containing TK*/PACKAGES files.
    /// Used to discover toolkit→module mappings for splitting.
    /// Relative to the config file directory.

```
/// (e.g., gp_Pnt, TopoDS_Shape) to avoid collisions and make the mapping obvious.
///
/// Returns the generated Rust code as a String.
pub fn generate_ffi(
    all_classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    all_functions: &[&crate::model::ParsedFunction],
    all_headers: &[String],
    collections: &[super::collections::CollectionInfo],
    symbol_table: &crate::resolver::SymbolTable,
    all_bindings: &[super::bindings::ClassBindings],
    function_bindings: &[super::bindings::FunctionBinding],
    handle_able_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    extra_typedef_names: &HashSet<String>,
    non_allocatable_classes: &HashSet<String>,
) -> (String, Vec<NestedTypeInfo>) {
    // Get all classes with protected destructors, and merge in non-allocatable classes
    // (non-allocatable classes should not get destructors/CppDeletable either)
    let mut protected_destructor_class_names = symbol_table.protected_destructor_class_names();
    for cls in non_allocatable_classes {
        protected_destructor_class_names.insert(cls.clone());
    }

    // All enum names (needed for opaque type filtering)
    let all_enum_names = &symbol_table.all_enum_names;

    // Collect collection type names to exclude from class generation
    // Collections are generated separately with specialized wrappers
    let collection_type_names: HashSet<String> = collections.iter()
        .map(|c| c.typedef_name.clone())
        .collect();

    // Emit ffi declarations from pre-computed ClassBindings
    let class_items: String = all_bindings
        .iter()
        .filter(|b| !collection_type_names.contains(&b.cpp_name))
        .map(|b| super::bindings::emit_ffi_class(b))
        .collect();

    // Generate namespace-level free functions from pre-computed FunctionBindings
    let function_items = generate_functions_from_bindings(function_bindings);

    // Generate Handle type declarations
    let handle_decls = generate_handle_declarations(all_classes, handle_able_classes, &symbol_table.handle_able_classes);

```
