```
/// Generate Handle type declarations
fn generate_handle_declarations(
    classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    handle_able_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    extra_handle_able: &HashSet<String>,
) -> String {
    let mut handles = BTreeSet::new();

    // Classes that are handle-able (transitively inherit from Standard_Transient)
    let mut defined_handles = BTreeSet::new();
    for class in classes {
        if handle_able_classes.contains(&class.name) {
            handles.insert(class.name.clone());
            defined_handles.insert(class.name.clone());
        }
    }

    // Also generate Handle declarations for types that appear in Handle(...)
    // in method signatures, even if their own headers are excluded.
    // This ensures that methods like GeomAPI_Interpolate(Handle(TColgp_HArray1OfPnt))
    // can be generated even when TColgp_HArray1OfPnt.hxx is excluded.
    for name in extra_handle_able {
        handles.insert(name.clone());
    }

    let mut out = String::new();
    for class_name in &handles {
        let handle_type_name = type_mapping::handle_type_name(class_name);
        writeln!(out, "/// Handle to {}", class_name).unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "#[repr(C)]").unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "pub struct {} {{ _opaque: [u8; 0] }}", handle_type_name).unwrap();
    }

    // For extra handle types (not from parsed classes), generate standalone
    // CppDeletable impls and destructor FFI declarations.
    // Parsed classes get these in their module files instead.
    let extra_handles: Vec<_> = handles.iter()
        .filter(|name| !defined_handles.contains(*name))
        .collect();
    if !extra_handles.is_empty() {
        writeln!(out).unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "extern \"C\" {{").unwrap();
        for class_name in &extra_handles {
            let handle_type_name = type_mapping::handle_type_name(class_name);
            writeln!(out, "    pub fn {}_destructor(ptr: *mut {});", handle_type_name, handle_type_name).unwrap();
        }
        writeln!(out, "}}").unwrap();
        writeln!(out).unwrap();
        for class_name in &extra_handles {
            let handle_type_name = type_mapping::handle_type_name(class_name);
            writeln!(out, "unsafe impl crate::CppDeletable for {} {{", handle_type_name).unwrap();
            writeln!(out, "    unsafe fn cpp_delete(ptr: *mut Self) {{").unwrap();
            writeln!(out, "        {}_destructor(ptr);", handle_type_name).unwrap();
            writeln!(out, "    }}").unwrap();
            writeln!(out, "}}").unwrap();
        }
    }

    out
}

/// Generate opaque type declarations
/// Extra destructor type info for destructor generation.
/// Covers nested types (cpp_name with ::, ffi_name with _) and extra typedef types (e.g. gp_Vec3f).
pub struct NestedTypeInfo {
    pub cpp_name: String,
    pub ffi_name: String,
}

fn generate_opaque_declarations(
    collected_types: &CollectedTypes,
    classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    all_enum_names: &HashSet<String>,
    protected_destructor_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    collection_type_names: &HashSet<String>,
    extra_typedef_names: &HashSet<String>,
) -> (String, Vec<NestedTypeInfo>) {
    let defined_classes: HashSet<_> = classes.iter().map(|c| c.name.clone()).collect();
    let mut out = String::new();

        if defined_classes.contains(type_name) {

        }
        if all_enum_names.contains(type_name) {
            continue;
```

```
pub fn generate_ffi(
    all_classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    all_functions: &[&crate::model::ParsedFunction],
    all_headers: &[String],
    collections: &[super::collections::CollectionInfo],
    symbol_table: &crate::resolver::SymbolTable,
    all_bindings: &[super::bindings::ClassBindings],
    function_bindings: &[super::bindings::FunctionBinding],
    handle_able_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    extra_typedef_names: &HashSet<String>,
    non_allocatable_classes: &HashSet<String>,
) -> (String, Vec<NestedTypeInfo>) {
    // Get all classes with protected destructors, and merge in non-allocatable classes
    // (non-allocatable classes should not get destructors/CppDeletable either)
    let mut protected_destructor_class_names = symbol_table.protected_destructor_class_names();
    for cls in non_allocatable_classes {
        protected_destructor_class_names.insert(cls.clone());
    }

    // All enum names (needed for opaque type filtering)
    let all_enum_names = &symbol_table.all_enum_names;

    // Collect collection type names to exclude from class generation
    // Collections are generated separately with specialized wrappers
    let collection_type_names: HashSet<String> = collections.iter()
        .map(|c| c.typedef_name.clone())
        .collect();

    // Emit ffi declarations from pre-computed ClassBindings
    let class_items: String = all_bindings
        .iter()
        .filter(|b| !collection_type_names.contains(&b.cpp_name))
        .map(|b| super::bindings::emit_ffi_class(b))
        .collect();

    // Generate namespace-level free functions from pre-computed FunctionBindings
    let function_items = generate_functions_from_bindings(function_bindings);

    // Generate Handle type declarations
    let handle_decls = generate_handle_declarations(all_classes, handle_able_classes, &symbol_table.handle_able_classes);

    // Collect opaque type declarations (types referenced but not defined)
    let collected_types = collect_referenced_types(all_classes, all_functions, handle_able_classes);
    let (opaque_type_decls, nested_types) = generate_opaque_declarations(
        &collected_types,
        all_classes,
        all_enum_names,
        &protected_destructor_class_names,
        &collection_type_names,
        extra_typedef_names,
    );

    // Generate destructor declarations for nested types and extra typedef types
    let nested_destructor_decls = if nested_types.is_empty() {
        String::new()
    } else {
        let mut s = String::new();
        writeln!(s).unwrap();
        writeln!(s, "    // ========================================").unwrap();
        writeln!(s, "    // Nested type & typedef type destructors").unwrap();
        writeln!(s, "    // ========================================").unwrap();
        writeln!(s).unwrap();
        for nt in &nested_types {
            writeln!(s, "    pub fn {}_destructor(self_: *mut {});", nt.ffi_name, nt.ffi_name).unwrap();
        }
        s
    };

    // Generate CppDeletable impls for nested types and extra typedef types
    let nested_deletable_impls = if nested_types.is_empty() {
        String::new()
    } else {
        let mut s = String::new();
        writeln!(s).unwrap();
        writeln!(s, "// CppDeletable impls for nested and typedef types").unwrap();
        for nt in &nested_types {
            writeln!(s, "unsafe impl crate::CppDeletable for {} {{", nt.ffi_name).unwrap();
            writeln!(s, "    unsafe fn cpp_delete(ptr: *mut Self) {{").unwrap();
            writeln!(s, "        {}_destructor(ptr);", nt.ffi_name).unwrap();
            writeln!(s, "    }}").unwrap();
            writeln!(s, "}}").unwrap();
        }
        s
    };

    // Build the output
    let mut out = String::new();

    // File header
    let header_count = all_headers.len();
    writeln!(out, "//! extern \"C\" FFI for OpenCASCADE").unwrap();
    writeln!(out, "//!").unwrap();
    writeln!(out, "//! This file was automatically generated by opencascade-bi
... (truncated, 5359 chars)
```

```
            writeln!(out, "    // ========================").unwrap();
            writeln!(out, "    // Free functions").unwrap();
            writeln!(out, "    // ========================").unwrap();
            writeln!(out).unwrap();
            out.push_str(&function_items);
        }

        out.push_str(&coll_ffi_decls);

        // Nested type destructor declarations
        out.push_str(&nested_destructor_decls);

        // Close extern "C" block
        writeln!(out, "}}").unwrap();

        // CppDeletable impls for nested types (must be after extern block)
```

```
    all_bindings: &[super::bindings::ClassBindings],

    // First generate the monolithic output to extract struct definitions
    let (monolithic, nested_types) = generate_ffi(
        all_classes, all_functions, all_headers, collections,
        symbol_table, all_bindings, function_bindings,
        handle_able_classes, extra_typedef_names, non_allocatable_classes,
    );

    // Split the monolithic output at the last extern "C" block
    // The struct defs are before it, the methods are inside it
    // Find the main extern "C" { block (the one containing class methods)
    // It's the last "extern \"C\" {" in the file
    let extern_pos = monolithic.rfind("\nextern \"C\" {").map(|p| p + 1);
    let (struct_defs, _extern_block) = if let Some(pos) = extern_pos {
        (&monolithic[..pos], &monolithic[pos..])
    } else {
        (monolithic.as_str(), "")
    };

    // Get all classes with protected destructors
    let mut protected_destructor_class_names = symbol_table.protected_destructor_class_names();
    for cls in non_allocatable_classes {
        protected_destructor_class_names.insert(cls.clone());
    }
    let collection_type_names: HashSet<String> = collections.iter()
        .map(|c| c.typedef_name.clone())
        .collect();

    // Helper to extract C++ module from name prefix
    let cpp_module = |name: &str| -> String {
        name.split('_').next().unwrap_or("").to_string()
    };

    // Group bindings by toolkit
    let mut tk_bindings: std::collections::HashMap<String, Vec<&super::bindings::ClassBindings>> =
        std::collections::HashMap::new();
    for b in all_bindings {
        if collection_type_names.contains(&b.cpp_name) {
            continue;
        }
        let module = cpp_module(&b.cpp_name);
        let toolkit = module_to_toolkit.get(&module)
            .cloned()
            .unwrap_or_else(|| "misc".to_string());
        tk_bindings.entry(toolkit).or_default().push(b);
    }

    // Group function bindings by toolkit
    let mut tk_functions: std::collections::HashMap<String, Vec<&super::bindings::FunctionBinding>> =
        std::collections::HashMap::new();
```

```

/// Generate free function declarations from pre-computed FunctionBindings.
fn generate_functions_from_bindings(
    function_bindings: &[super::bindings::FunctionBinding],
) -> String {
    let mut out = String::new();
    for func in function_bindings {
        let params_str: String = func.params.iter()
            .map(|p| format!("{}: {}", p.rust_name, p.rust_ffi_type))
            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
            .join(", ");

        let source_attr = format_source_attribution(
            &func.source_header,
            func.source_line,
            &format!("{}::{}", func.namespace, func.short_name),
        );
        writeln!(out, "    /// {}", source_attr).unwrap();
        if let Some(ref rt) = func.return_type {
            writeln!(out, "    pub fn {}({}) -> crate::OcctResult<{}>;", func.cpp_wrapper_name, params_str, rt.rust_ffi_type).unwrap();
        } else {
            writeln!(out, "    pub fn {}({}) -> *const std::ffi::c_char;", func.cpp_wrapper_name, params_str).unwrap();
        }
    }
    out
}

```
</details>

1. Call  to get monolithic output

3. Re-generate the main extern block as per-toolkit files (using  and `generate_functions_from_bindings` directly)

```
        let handle_destructors = generate_handle_destructor_declarations(all_classes, handle_able_classes, &symbol_table.handle_able_classes);
        if !handle_destructors.is_empty() {
            writeln!(out, "// Generated by opencascade-binding-generator").unwrap();
            writeln!(out, "// FFI extern block for handle destructors").unwrap();
            writeln!(out).unwrap();
            writeln!(out, "extern \"C\" {{").unwrap();
            out.push_str(&handle_destructors);
            writeln!(out, "}}").unwrap();
        }
        out
    };

    // Build ffi.rs facade
    let mut facade = String::new();
    facade.push_str(struct_defs);

    // Include handle destructor block
    if !handle_destructor_block.is_empty() {
        writeln!(facade, "include!(\"ffi_extern_handles.rs\");").unwrap();
        toolkit_files.push(("__handles".to_string(), handle_destructor_block));
    }

    // Include per-toolkit extern blocks
    for toolkit in &sorted_toolkits {
        writeln!(facade, "include!(\"ffi_extern_{}.rs\");", toolkit).unwrap();
    }

    // Include collection extern block
    if let Some(coll_code) = coll_extern {
        writeln!(facade, "include!(\"ffi_extern_collections.rs\");").unwrap();
        toolkit_files.push(("__collections".to_string(), coll_code));
    }

    // CppDeletable impls for nested types (after all extern blocks)
    if !nested_types.is_empty() {
        writeln!(facade).unwrap();
        writeln!(facade, "// CppDeletable impls for nested and typedef types").unwrap();
        for nt in &nested_types {
            writeln!(facade, "unsafe impl crate::CppDeletable for {} {{", nt.ffi_name).unwrap();
            writeln!(facade, "    unsafe fn cpp_delete(ptr: *mut Self) {{").unwrap();
            writeln!(facade, "        {}_destructor(ptr);", nt.ffi_name).unwrap();
            writeln!(facade, "    }}").unwrap();
            writeln!(facade, "}}").unwrap();
        }
    }

    (facade, nested_types, toolkit_files)
}
```
</details>

```
/// Generate free function declarations from pre-computed FunctionBindings.
fn generate_functions_from_bindings(
    function_bindings: &[super::bindings::FunctionBinding],
) -> String {
    let mut out = String::new();
    for func in function_bindings {
        let params_str: String = func.params.iter()
            .map(|p| format!("{}: {}", p.rust_name, p.rust_ffi_type))
            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
            .join(", ");

        let source_attr = format_source_attribution(
            &func.source_header,
            func.source_line,
            &format!("{}::{}", func.namespace, func.short_name),
        );
        writeln!(out, "    /// {}", source_attr).unwrap();
        if let Some(ref rt) = func.return_type {
            writeln!(out, "    pub fn {}({}) -> crate::OcctResult<{}>;", func.cpp_wrapper_name, params_str, rt.rust_ffi_type).unwrap();
        } else {
            writeln!(out, "    pub fn {}({}) -> *const std::ffi::c_char;", func.cpp_wrapper_name, params_str).unwrap();
        }
    }
    out
}

/// Generate Handle type declarations
fn generate_handle_declarations(
    classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    handle_able_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    extra_handle_able: &HashSet<String>,
) -> String {
    let mut handles = BTreeSet::new();

    // Classes that are handle-able (transitively inherit from Standard_Transient)
    let mut defined_handles = BTreeSet::new();
    for class in classes {
        if handle_able_classes.contains(&class.name) {
            handles.insert(class.name.clone());
            defined_handles.insert(class.name.clone());
        }
    }

    // Also generate Handle declarations for types that appear in Handle(...)
    // in method signatures, even if their own headers are excluded.
    // This ensures that methods like GeomAPI_Interpolate(Handle(TColgp_HArray1OfPnt))
    // can be generated even when TColgp_HArray1OfPnt.hxx is excluded.
```
</details>

```
pub fn generate_rust_ffi_collections(collections: &[CollectionInfo]) -> (String, String) {
    if collections.is_empty() {
        return (String::new(), String::new());
    }
    
    // Generate opaque struct declarations (outside extern "C" block)
    let mut type_decls = String::new();
    type_decls.push_str("// ========================\n");
    type_decls.push_str("// Collection types (opaque)\n");
    type_decls.push_str("// ========================\n\n");
    for info in collections {
        writeln!(type_decls, "#[repr(C)]").unwrap();
        writeln!(type_decls, "pub struct {} {{ _opaque: [u8; 0] }}", info.typedef_name).unwrap();
        // Iterator type
        let iter_name = format!("{}Iterator", info.short_name);
        writeln!(type_decls, "#[repr(C)]").unwrap();
        writeln!(type_decls, "pub struct {} {{ _opaque: [u8; 0] }}", iter_name).unwrap();
    }
    type_decls.push('\n');
    
    let mut ffi_decls = String::new();
    
    ffi_decls.push_str("    // ========================\n");
    ffi_decls.push_str("    // Collection type wrappers\n");
    ffi_decls.push_str("    // ========================\n\n");
    
    for info in collections {
        ffi_decls.push_str(&generate_rust_ffi_collection(info));
    }
    
    (type_decls, ffi_decls)
}

/// Generate Rust FFI declarations for a single collection
/// Uses full C++ type names (e.g., TopoDS_Shape, TopTools_ListOfShape)
fn generate_rust_ffi_collection(info: &CollectionInfo) -> String {
    match info.kind {
        CollectionKind::Array1 => return generate_rust_ffi_array1(info),
        CollectionKind::Array2 => return generate_rust_ffi_array2(info),
        _ => {}
    }

    let mut output = String::new();
    let coll_name = &info.typedef_name;
    let iter_name = format!("{}Iterator", info.short_name);
    
    // Constructor
    output.push_str(&format!("    /// Create a new empty {}\n", coll_name));
    output.push_str(&format!("    pub fn {}_new() -> *mut {};\n\n", coll_name, coll_name));
    
    // Destructor
    output.push_str(&format!("    /// Destroy a {}\n", coll_name));
    output.push_str(&format!("    pub fn {}_destructor(self_: *mut {});\n\n", coll_name, coll_name));
    
    // Size method
    output.push_str(&format!("    /// Get number of elements in {}\n", coll_name));
    output.push_str(&format!("    pub fn {}_size(coll: *const {}) -> i32;\n\n", coll_name, coll_name));
    
    // Clear method
    output.push_str(&format!("    /// Remove all elements from {}\n", coll_name));
    output.push_str(&format!("    pub fn {}_clear(coll: *mut {});\n\n", coll_name, coll_name));
    
    // Add/append method based on collection kind
    match info.kind {
        CollectionKind::List => {
            output.push_str("    /// Append an element to the list\n");
            output.push_str(&format!("    pub fn {}_append(coll: *mut {}, item: *const {});\n\n", coll_name, coll_name, info.element_type));
            output.push_str("    /// Prepend an element to the list\n");
```

```
            .push(b);
```
</details>

Now I see the structure. The `module_to_toolkit` is computed inside the `if split_config.cpp_split...` block. I need to either:
1. Compute `module_to_toolkit` outside both blocks when `ffi_split` is set, OR
2. Compute it separately for ffi_split

The cleanest approach: compute `module_to_toolkit` before the ffi.rs generation when either `cpp_split` or `ffi_split` is "toolkit". Let me reorganize the code:

```
        all_classes,
```

```
[split]
# Strategy for C++ wrapper splitting: "toolkit" splits into one file per OCCT toolkit.
cpp_split = "toolkit"
# Path to OCCT source directory containing TK*/PACKAGES files (relative to this file).
occt_source_dir = "../occt-sys/OCCT/src"
"NCollection_Shared<NCollection_IndexedMap<opencascade::handle<SelectMgr_EntityOwner>>>" = { header = "NCollection_Shared.hxx", module = "NCollection", handle = true }
```

Now I also need to make sure the `regenerate-bindings.sh` script cleans up the new `ffi_extern_*.rs` files. Let me check:

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 1-30 of <a href="../scripts/regenerate-bindings.sh#L1-L31">regenerate-bindings.sh</a></summary>

```
#!/bin/bash
# Regenerate OpenCASCADE bindings
#
# This script regenerates the FFI bindings for opencascade-sys.
# Run from the repository root.
#
# Usage:
#   ./scripts/regenerate-bindings.sh
#
# Requirements:
#   - Xcode command line tools (for libclang)
#   - OCCT headers built in target/OCCT/include

set -euo pipefail

# Repository root (script assumes it's run from repo root)
REPO_ROOT="${REPO_ROOT:-$(pwd)}"
cd "$REPO_ROOT"

# Paths
OCCT_INCLUDE="$REPO_ROOT/target/OCCT/include"
OUTPUT_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/crates/opencascade-sys/generated"
CONFIG_FILE="$REPO_ROOT/crates/opencascade-sys/bindings.toml"

# Check prerequisites
if [[ ! -d "$OCCT_INCLUDE" ]]; then
    echo "Error: OCCT include directory not found at $OCCT_INCLUDE"
    echo "Run 'cargo build -p occt-sys' first to build OCCT"
    exit 1
fi
```
</details>

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 30-60 of <a href="../scripts/regenerate-bindings.sh#L30-L61">regenerate-bindings.sh</a></summary>

```
fi

if [[ ! -f "$CONFIG_FILE" ]]; then
    echo "Error: Config file not found at $CONFIG_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

# Build the generator in release mode to avoid a UB crash in the `clang` crate
# (v2.0.0) that only manifests in debug builds on Rust >= 1.78. See
# crates/opencascade-binding-generator/Cargo.toml for details.
echo "Building binding generator..."
cargo build --release -p opencascade-binding-generator

echo "Generating bindings from $CONFIG_FILE..."

# Clean generated directory
echo "Cleaning $OUTPUT_DIR..."
rm -f "$OUTPUT_DIR"/*.rs "$OUTPUT_DIR"/*.hxx "$OUTPUT_DIR"/*.cpp

# Set library path for libclang on macOS
if [[ "$(uname)" == "Darwin" ]]; then
    XCODE_TOOLCHAIN="$(xcode-select -p)/Toolchains/XcodeDefault.xctoolchain/usr/lib"
    export DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH="$XCODE_TOOLCHAIN:${DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH:-}"
fi

# Run the generator
"$REPO_ROOT/target/release/occt-bindgen" \
    --config "$CONFIG_FILE" \
    -I "$OCCT_INCLUDE" \
    -o "$OUTPUT_DIR" \
    "$@"
```
</details>

Line 47 already cleans `*.rs` which covers `ffi_extern_*.rs`. Good.

Now let me also check that the build.rs in opencascade-sys doesn't need any changes - the ffi_extern_*.rs files are `include!()`'d from ffi.rs so they're compiled as part of ffi.rs, not as separate compilation units. The build.rs only compiles C++ files. Let me verify:

Searched for regex `ffi|\.rs` (`**/crates/opencascade-sys/build.rs`), 1 result
- [build.rs:120](../crates/opencascade-sys/build.rs#L120)

Good - `crates/opencascade-sys/build.rs` doesn't mention .rs files. The Rust compilation is handled by cargo itself through lib.rs → ffi.rs → include!() macros.

Wait - actually the point of splitting ffi.rs was to enable parallel codegen. Let me check if rustc is actually using codegen units effectively. The split doesn't help with parsing but might help with codegen. However, `include!()` doesn't create separate compilation units - it's just textual inclusion. The real benefit would come from splitting into separate crates or using separate `mod` files that each have their own `extern "C"` block. But since all per-module .rs files reference `crate::ffi::ClassName`, they depend on ffi.rs types. The `include!()` approach is correct for now.

## Final Report

### Build Performance Summary

| Commit | Description | Build Time | Peak RSS |
|--------|-------------|-----------|----------|
| `77d6fb4` (baseline) | Monolithic wrappers.cpp + ffi.rs | ~70s | 6.6 GB |
| `b5624ea` (Phase 1) | Split wrappers.cpp → 50 per-toolkit .cpp files | 71s | 7.0 GB |
| `f8d8154` (Phase 2) | Split ffi.rs → 42K facade + 51 per-toolkit extern files | 71s | 6.8 GB |

### What changed

**Phase 1** (`b5624ea`): Split the 649K-line monolithic ## Final Report

### Build Performance Summary

| Commit | Description | Build Time | Peak RSS |
|--------|-------------|-----------|----------|
| `77d6fb4` (baseline) | Monolithic wrappers.cpp + ffi.rs | ~70s | 6.6 GB |
| `b5624ea` (Phase 1) | Split wrappers.cpp → 50 per-toolkit .cpp files | 71s | 7.0 GB |
| `f8d8154` (Phase 2) | Split ffi.rs → 42K facade + 51 per-toolkit extern files | 71s | 6.8 GB |

### What changed

**Phase 1** (`b5624ea`): Split the 649K-line monolithic `wrappers.cpp` into 50 per-toolkit C++ files + a shared preamble header. The `cc` crate automatically parallelizes compilation of multiple `.file()` calls. Build time didn't improve because the C++ compilation time is dominated by header parsing (every file includes the same heavy OCCT headers via the preamble).

**Phase 2** (`f8d8154`): Split the 751K-line monolithic `ffi.rs` into a 42K-line facade (struct definitions + `include!()` macros) and 51 per-toolkit `ffi_extern_*.rs` files containing separate `extern "C"` blocks. Build time is unchanged because `include!()` is textual expansion — rustc still processes all the same code in one compilation unit.

### Key structural improvement

Both splits are **maintainability wins** even without build time improvement:
- Generated file diffs are now per-toolkit instead of monolithic
- Individual toolkit files are manageable sizes (15K–930K bytes vs 649K/751K lines)
- Configuration is driven by `crates/opencascade-sys/bindings.toml` (, )

### Why build time didn't improve

The bottleneck is **not** parallelization of the generated code — it's the OCCT C++ headers. Each per-toolkit .cpp file still needs to `#include` heavy OCCT headers, and the header parsing dominates. On the Rust side, `include!()` is just textual substitution so rustc still compiles everything as one unit. Real parallelization would require precompiled headers (C++ side) or separate crates (Rust side).

## User (5)

> Why didn&#x27;t you use pre-compiled headers? Why didn&#x27;t you compile the ffi_*.rs files separately and instead include!() them in ffi.rs? Just answer, don&#x27;t change anything.

*2026-02-26 07:47*

### Assistant — Claude Sonnet 4.6

> 💭 The user is asking two questions:
> 1. Why didn't I use pre-compiled headers (PCH) for the C++ split?
> 2. Why did I use `include!()` to inline the ffi_*.rs files instead of compiling them as separate Rust modules?
> 
> Let me think about these carefully.
> 
> **Pre-compiled headers:**
> PCH would help amortize the cost of parsing heavy OCCT headers across the 50 per-toolkit .cpp files. The issue is that the `cc` crate doesn't support PCH natively, and setting up PCH requires:
> 1. Creating a PCH file (clang: `-x c++-header -o preamble.pch preamble.hxx`)
> 
> 
> 2. Using it with `-include-pch preamble.pch` for each .cpp file
> 3. Making sure the PCH is built first and each file uses the exact same compile flags
> 
> The `cc` crate lacks native PCH support, so I'd need to manually orchestrate this through `build.rs` with raw `Command` invocations. Plus, macOS Clang has strict requirements—the PCH must be generated with identical flags as the source files. Since AGENTS.md doesn't mention PCH and the `cc` crate doesn't provide it out of the box, I went with the simpler approach of just splitting the compilation.

**Pre-compiled headers:** The `cc` crate has no PCH support. You'd need to manually invoke clang to build a `.pch` file first, then pass `-include-pch` to each subsequent compilation via custom `crates/opencascade-sys/build.rs` scripting using raw `Command`s or `cc`'s `.flag()` escape hatch, while ensuring the PCH was generated with identical flags. It's doable but non-trivial, and I didn't attempt it. That's the actual fix for the C++ build time.

**`include!()` instead of separate modules:** The 321 per-module `.rs` files (e.g., `ais.rs`,  scripting using raw `Command`s or `cc`'s `.flag()` escape hatch, while ensuring the PCH was generated with identical flags. It's doable but non-trivial, and I didn't attempt it. That's the actual fix for the C++ build time.

**`include!()` instead of separate modules:** The 321 per-module `.rs` files (e.g., `ais.rs`, `gp.rs`) all reference types as `crate::ffi::Foo`. If the ffi extern blocks were separate Rust `mod`s — e.g., `mod ffi_TKernel { extern "C" { ... } }` — the type paths would change to `crate::ffi::ffi_TKernel::Foo`, breaking every reference. There are ~13,000 `crate::ffi::` references across those 321 files.

`include!()` keeps everything flat in the `ffi` namespace so all existing code continues to work unchanged. The cost is that rustc still sees one monolithic compilation unit — no parallelism gain.

To get actual parallel Rust codegen you'd need to make ffi a module hierarchy with `pub use ffi::TK*::*` re-exports in ffi/mod.rs, updating all the generated module files — or split into separate crates, which brings cross-crate dependency complexity. Both approaches require significantly more invasive changes.

### Assistant — Claude Opus 4.6

> 💭 This is a major restructuring task. Let me break it down:
> 
> 1. Instead of `include!()` in ffi.rs, make each `ffi_extern_*.rs` a proper module
> 2. Update the 321 per-module .rs files to reference `crate::ffi_extern_TKxxx::Foo` instead of `crate::ffi::Foo`
> 3. Rename ffi.rs to something clearer (it contains type definitions - structs, handles, etc.)
> 4. Update lib.rs to declare all the new modules
> 5. Measure compile time
> 6. Commit
> 
> Let me think about the architecture:
> 
> Current structure:
> 
> 
> - `ffi.rs`: struct defs (handles, opaque types, POD structs) + `include!("ffi_extern_*.rs")` for each toolkit
> - `ffi_extern_TK*.rs`: per-toolkit `extern "C" { ... }` blocks
> - Per-module files (gp.rs, ais.rs, etc.): reference `crate::ffi::Foo`
> 
> New structure:
> - `ffi_types.rs` (or similar): struct defs only (handles, opaque types, POD structs, CppDeletable impls)
> - `ffi_extern_TK*.rs`: standalone modules with `extern "C" { ... }` blocks - they need to reference types from `ffi_types`
> - Per-module files: reference types from `crate::ffi_types::Foo` for struct types, and function declarations from `crate::ffi_extern_TKxxx::func_name`
> 
> The tricky part is that per-module files currently reference both types and functions as `crate::ffi::*` — types like `gp_Pnt` and functions like `gp_Pnt_ctor()`. When I split this, types move to `ffi_types` but functions scatter across different `ffi_extern_TK*` modules, so each per-module file needs to know which toolkit owns each function.
> 
> I could use re-exports in a central `ffi` module to re-export everything from `ffi_types` and all the toolkit modules, but that defeats the parallelism goal since the ffi module would still depend on everything. Instead, I should follow the user's suggestion: have per-module files directly reference `crate::ffi_TKxxx::function_name` for functions and `crate::ffi_types::TypeName` for types. The binding generator already groups functions by toolkit, so during generation I can track which toolkit each function belongs to and update the per-module references accordingly.
> 
> The key insight is that the `ffi_extern_TK*.rs` files contain the `extern "C"` blocks with function declarations, and they need to import types from a separate `ffi_types.rs` module. So I'd rename the current `ffi.rs` to `ffi_types.rs` (keeping only struct definitions), make each `ffi_extern_TK*.rs` a proper module that imports those types, update the per-module generated files to reference the correct toolkit module for each function, and declare all these as top-level modules in `lib.rs` for true compilation parallelism. Now I need to figure out how the per-module files are currently generated and what references they contain, so I can update them to use the new split structure with toolkit-specific extern modules.

```
pub fn generate_module_reexports(
    module_name: &str,
    _rust_module_name: &str,
    _classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    collections: &[&super::collections::CollectionInfo],
    symbol_table: &crate::resolver::SymbolTable,
    module_bindings: &[&super::bindings::ClassBindings],
    module_fn_bindings: &[&super::bindings::FunctionBinding],
    module_skipped_fns: &[&super::bindings::SkippedSymbol],
    extra_types: &[(String, String)], // (ffi_name, short_name) for types not covered by ClassBindings
) -> String {
    let mut output = String::new();

    // File header
    output.push_str(&format!(
        "//! {} module re-exports\n//!\n//! This file was automatically generated by opencascade-binding-generator.\n//! Do not edit this file directly.\n\n",
        module_name
    ));

    output.push_str("#![allow(dead_code)]\n");
    output.push_str("#![allow(non_snake_case)]\n\n");

    // Generate re-exports for free functions from pre-computed FunctionBindings.
    for func in module_fn_bindings {
        // All free functions become real wrapper functions (not pub use re-exports)
        // so IDE "go to definition" lands in the public module.
        emit_free_function_wrapper(&mut output, func);
    }

    if !module_fn_bindings.is_empty() {
        output.push('\n');
    }

    // Generate Rust enum definitions for enums in this module
    let rust_module = crate::module_graph::module_to_rust_name(module_name);
    if let Some(enum_ids) = symbol_table.enums_by_module.get(&rust_module) {
        for enum_id in enum_ids {
            if let Some(resolved_enum) = symbol_table.enums.get(enum_id) {
                if !matches!(resolved_enum.status, crate::resolver::BindingStatus::Included) {
                    continue;
                }
                emit_rust_enum(&mut output, resolved_enum);
            }
        }
    }

    // Re-export collection types belonging to this module
    for coll in collections {
        output.push_str(&format!(
            "pub use crate::ffi::{} as {};\n",
            coll.typedef_name, coll.short_name
        ));
    }
    if !collections.is_empty() {
        output.push('\n');
    }

    // Generate impl blocks for collection types
    for coll in collections {
        output.push_str(&emit_collection_impl(coll));
    }

    // Group pre-computed bindings by source header for organized output
    use std::collections::BTreeMap;
    let mut bindings_by_header: BTreeMap<String, Vec<&super::bindings::ClassBindings>> =
        BTreeMap::new();
    for b in module_bindings {
        bindings_by_header
            .entry(b.source_header.clone())
            .or_default()
            .push(b);
    }

    // Generate re-exports and impl blocks for classes, grouped by header
    // Collect all handle types that are directly re-exported (derived handles with to_handle),
    // so we can avoid duplicating their re-export when they appear as upcast targets.
    let mut directly_exported_handles: std::collections::HashSet<String> = std::collections::HashSet::new();
    for b in module_bindings {
        if b.has_to_handle || b.has_handle_get {
            let handle_type_name = type_mapping::handle_type_name(&b.cpp_name);
            directly_exported_handles.insert(handle_type_name);
        }
    }

    // Also collect handle types referenced by upcast/downcast methods that need re-exporting.
    // These are handle types for base classes (upcast targets) or derived classes (downcast targets)
    // that external crates need to name.
    let mut base_handle_reexports: std::collections::BTreeSet<String> = std::collections::BTreeSet::new();
    for b in module_bindings {
        for hu in &b.handle_upcasts {
            if !directly_exported_handles.contains(&hu.base_handle_name) {
                base_handle_reexports.insert(hu.base_handle_name.clone());
            }
        }
        for hd in &b.handle_downcasts {
            if !directly_exported_handles.contains(&hd.derived_handle_name) {
```

```
/// Generate an `impl` block for a collection type, re-exporting its FFI helper functions as methods.
fn emit_collection_impl(coll: &super::collections::CollectionInfo) -> String {
    use super::collections::CollectionKind;
    let mut out = String::new();
    let coll_name = &coll.typedef_name;
    let short = &coll.short_name;
    let elem = &coll.element_type;

    out.push_str(&format!("impl {} {{\n", short));

    match coll.kind {
        CollectionKind::List | CollectionKind::Sequence => {
            // new
            out.push_str(&format!(
                "    /// Create a new empty {}\n    pub fn new() -> crate::OwnedPtr<Self> {{\n        unsafe {{ crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw(crate::ffi::{}_new()) }}\n    }}\n\n",
                short, coll_name
            ));
            // size
            out.push_str(&format!(
                "    /// Get number of elements\n    pub fn size(&self) -> i32 {{\n        unsafe {{ crate::ffi::{}_size(self as *const Self) }}\n    }}\n\n",
                coll_name
            ));
            // clear
            out.push_str(&format!(
                "    /// Remove all elements\n    pub fn clear(&mut self) {{\n        unsafe {{ crate::ffi::{}_clear(self as *mut Self) }}\n    }}\n\n",
                coll_name
            ));
            // append
            out.push_str(&format!(
                "    /// Append an element\n    pub fn append(&mut self, item: &crate::ffi::{}) {{\n        unsafe {{ crate::ffi::{}_append(self as *mut Self, item as *const crate::ffi::{}) }}\n    }}\n\n",
                elem, coll_name, elem
```

```
pub fn emit_reexport_class(bindings: &ClassBindings, module_name: &str) -> String {
    let cn = &bindings.cpp_name;
    let short_name = &bindings.short_name;

    let mut output = String::new();

    // Source attribution + doc comment for the class
    let class_source = format_source_attribution(
        &bindings.source_header,
        bindings.source_line,
        cn,
    );
    output.push_str(&format!("/// {}\n", class_source));
    if let Some(ref comment) = bindings.doc_comment {
        for line in comment.lines() {
            let trimmed = line.trim();
            if trimmed.is_empty() {
                output.push_str("///\n");
            } else {
                output.push_str(&format!("/// {}\n", trimmed));
            }
        }
    }

    // Type alias re-export
    output.push_str(&format!(
        "pub use crate::ffi::{} as {};\n\n",
        cn, short_name
    ));

    // POD structs are Copy types with real fields.
    // They still need CppDeletable because other classes may return them by pointer.
    if bindings.is_pod_struct {
        output.push_str(&format!(
            "unsafe impl crate::CppDeletable for {} {{\n    unsafe fn cpp_delete(ptr: *mut Self) {{\n        crate::ffi::{}_destructor(ptr);\n    }}\n}}\n\n",
            short_name, cn
        ));
        return output;
    }

    // CppDeletable impl (unless protected destructor)
    if !bindings.has_protected_destructor {
        output.push_str(&format!(
            "unsafe impl crate::CppDeletable for {} {{\n    unsafe fn cpp_delete(ptr: *mut Self) {{\n        crate::ffi::{}_destructor(ptr);\n    }}\n}}\n\n",
            short_name, cn
        ));
    }

    // Build impl methods
    let mut impl_methods: Vec<String> = Vec::new();

    // 1. Constructors
    for ctor in &bindings.constructors {
        let params: Vec<String> = ctor
            .params
            .iter()
            .map(|p| format!("{}: {}", p.rust_name, p.rust_reexport_type))
            .collect();
        let args: Vec<String> = ctor.params.iter().map(|p| convert_arg(p)).collect();

        let source_attr = format_source_attribution(
            &bindings.source_header,
            ctor.source_line,
            &format!("{}::{}()", cn, cn),
        );
        let doc = format_reexport_doc(&source_attr, &ctor.doc_comment);

        if let Some(ref conv) = ctor.convenience_of {
            // Convenience constructor: Rust-only wrapper that delegates to full-arg version
            // Use raw param names (no CString conversion) since the target method handles it
            let convenience_args: Vec<String> = ctor.params.iter().map(|p| p.rust_name.clone()).collect();
            let mut all_args = convenience_args;
            all_args.extend(conv.default_exprs.iter().cloned());
            let unsafe_kw = if ctor.is_unsafe { "unsafe " } else { "" };
            impl_methods.push(format!(
                "{}    pub {}fn {}({}) -> crate::OwnedPtr<Self> {{\n        Self::{}({})\n    }}\n",
                doc,
                unsafe_kw,
```

> 💭 OK, so there are ~79 places in the generator code that emit `crate::ffi::`. I need to change these to either `crate::ffi_types::` (for types) or `crate::ffi_extern_TKxxx::` (for functions).
> 
> But wait - this is a lot more complex than just a search-and-replace. Each function call needs to know which toolkit it belongs to. The generator knows the module/class, and from there we can look up the toolkit. But the per-module .rs generation doesn't currently have access to the module_to_toolkit mapping.
> 
> Let me think about the architecture more carefully.
>  
> I'm considering a simpler approach: create a new `ffi` module that re-exports everything from `ffi_types` and all the `ffi_extern_*.rs` files, so `crate::ffi::` still works without changing the generated code. But that wouldn't actually help with parallel compilation since the re-export module would still need to depend on all of them, making it the bottleneck.
> 
> Let me think through Rust's actual compilation model: parsing and type checking happen across the whole crate at once, while codegen parallelism is controlled by `codegen-units` settings, not module structure. So splitting into separate modules versus using `include!()` shouldn't make a difference in total compilation time. The user believes it will be faster though, so I should implement it and measure to see if there's any actual benefit—or if the measurement reveals that the approach doesn't help, that's useful information too.
> 
> Looking at what the user actually wants: they're asking me to stop using the re-export facade pattern, move the extern blocks into separate compiled modules under `ffi/`, and have those modules reference types via `crate::ffi_TKxxx::Foo` instead of centralizing everything. This means creating a `ffi/mod.rs` that declares submodules for each extern block file, then moving or symlinking those files into the ffi directory so they compile as separate modules rather than being textually included into one giant file.
> 
> The key insight is that I need to thread toolkit information through the code generation so that when emitting references to types and functions, I can use the correct module path—`crate::ffi_types::` for struct definitions and `crate::ffi_extern_TKxxx::` for the actual extern function calls. I can parameterize this by passing prefix strings into the emission functions rather than hardcoding `crate::ffi::` everywhere. I'm realizing the cleanest approach is to use imports at the top of each per-module file so I can reference types and functions without repeating the full path every time. I'll set up imports like `use crate::ffi_types as ffi` and `use crate::ffi_extern_{toolkit}` so that all the references within a module file can stay simple and consistent. I need to figure out how to handle the dual nature of these references—types need one prefix while function calls need another. Since the generator already knows which is which when it creates them, I can thread `type_prefix` and `fn_prefix` parameters through the functions that emit code. For `emit_reexport_class()`, `emit_free_function_wrapper()`, and the collection-related functions, I'll replace hardcoded `"crate::ffi"` strings with the appropriate prefix based on context. The tricky part is that collections currently live in their own extern block, so I need to decide whether to keep them there or distribute them to their respective toolkits—keeping them separate seems cleaner for now. I'm categorizing where all the CppDeletable implementations need to live: regular classes get them in their per-module files with destructors from the toolkit extern blocks, extra handles have them in ffi_types.rs alongside their small handle extern block, and nested types need to move from the facade to ffi_types.rs with qualified calls to the collection extern functions.
> 
> Since Rust modules within a crate can reference each other freely without DAG constraints, I can safely put the nested type CppDeletable impls in ffi_types.rs and have them call destructors from ffi_extern_collections without creating a circular dependency. The extra handle destructors are already defined in ffi_types.rs itself, so those don't need external references.
> 
> Now I'm mapping out the overall module structure: ffi_types.rs will contain all the struct definitions and CppDeletable implementations, while ffi_extern_*.rs files will hold the extern "C" blocks organized by toolkit, and per-module files will re-export types and call the appropriate FFI functions based on which module they belong to.
> 
> The implementation needs to thread a function prefix parameter through several key functions so that each generated file knows whether to reference `crate::ffi_extern_TKMath::` or `crate::ffi_extern_collections::` depending on context. I could use a search-and-replace approach on the output strings, but that's fragile—it's cleaner to pass the prefix explicitly through the code generation functions and update all the format strings that currently hardcode `crate::ffi::`.
> 
> The main changes are: updating `generate_ffi_split()` to output ffi_types.rs without include macros, modifying `emit_reexport_class()` and the free function wrappers to accept and use the fn_prefix parameter, updating collection implementations to reference the collections module, and finally generating lib.rs with all the module declarations.
> 
> I should also make sure the non-split path (monolithic ffi.rs) continues to work unchanged—these modifications only apply when generating per-module files.
> 
> The cleanest approach is to pass module path information as parameters: in non-split mode, use `"crate::ffi"` for everything, but in split mode, use separate paths for types (`"crate::ffi_types"`) and functions (toolkit-specific like `"crate::ffi_extern_TKMath"`). I'm thinking of defining a struct to encapsulate these paths so they can be passed around cleanly to the code generation functions.
> 
> Defining the struct... I'm realizing that CppDeletable impls are only emitted for classes in their own module, so cross-module type references are just type paths that don't need function lookups. The only references between modules would be type uses in signatures, which resolve to `crate::ffi_types`. Now I'm counting the scope of changes needed across the generator functions and deciding to use a struct to hold the module path information rather than threading multiple parameters through the call stack.

> 💭 That's a LOT of references. Let me count them: roughly 50+ references in rust.rs and ~29 in bindings.rs, totaling ~80+ references.The key insight: in the generated per-module .rs files, there are two categories:
> 1. **Type references**: `pub use crate::ffi::TypeName as ShortName;`, `&crate::ffi::TypeName`, `crate::OwnedPtr<crate::ffi::TypeName>`
> 2. **Function calls**: `crate::ffi::func_name(args)`, `crate::ffi::TypeName_destructor(ptr)`Both are currently emit as `crate::ffi::`. We need to split them into:
> 
> 
> - Type refs → `crate::ffi_types::`
> - Function calls → `crate::ffi_extern_{toolkit}::` or `crate::ffi_extern_collections::`
> 
> Rather than modifying 80+ format strings individually, I could pass a struct with module paths through the generator functions and use local variables to swap the prefix in each format string. The simplest approach might be a post-generation string replacement that distinguishes type references from function calls based on their patterns in the output.
> 
> Looking at the generated code, I can identify the difference: function references always end up with a `(` after the name (like `Foo_destructor(` or `Foo_method(`), while type references don't. The challenge is that simple regex won't reliably distinguish between a type name like `Foo_ctor` followed by `(` versus an actual function call pattern.
> 
> I'm realizing the regex approach is too fragile here. Instead, I should use a struct-based approach to track FFI paths more explicitly.
> 
> Defining a struct to manage FFI type and function references...
> 
> Now I need to audit all the format strings throughout the codebase and classify each `crate::ffi::` reference as either a type, function, or collection function reference, then swap them to use the appropriate path field from the struct. I'm seeing the scope of this refactoring now—there are roughly 80+ format string changes scattered across multiple functions. I could thread the `FfiPaths` struct through everything for precision, but that's a lot of edits. A cleaner approach would be to create helper functions that wrap the path logic, so instead of changing every format string individually, I just swap out the prefix generation in one place.

```
/// Context for resolving C++ class names to their public re-exported Rust type
/// paths instead of raw `crate::ffi::` paths.
pub struct ReexportTypeContext<'a> {
    /// Maps C++ class name (original, may contain ::) → (rust_module_name, short_name)
    pub class_public_info: &'a HashMap<String, (String, String)>,
    /// The Rust module name of the class/function currently being generated
    pub current_module_rust: String,
}

impl<'a> ReexportTypeContext<'a> {
    fn resolve_class(&self, cpp_name: &str) -> String {
        if let Some((module_rust, short)) = self.class_public_info.get(cpp_name) {
            if *module_rust == self.current_module_rust {
                short.clone()
            } else {
                format!("crate::{}::{}", module_rust, short)
            }
        } else {
            format!("crate::ffi::{}", Type::ffi_safe_class_name(cpp_name))
        }
    }
}
```
</details>

```
}

/// Build the class_public_info map from a list of parsed classes.
/// Maps C++ class name → (rust_module_name, short_name).
pub(crate) fn build_class_public_info(all_classes: &[&ParsedClass]) -> HashMap<String, (String, String)> {
    all_classes
        .iter()
        .map(|c| {
            let ffi_name = c.name.replace("::", "_");
            let module_rust = crate::module_graph::module_to_rust_name(&c.module);
            let short = crate::type_mapping::safe_short_name(&crate::type_mapping::short_name_for_module(&ffi_name, &c.module));
            (c.name.clone(), (module_rust, short))
        })
        .collect()
}

/// Convert a Type to Rust type string for re-export files
fn type_to_rust_string(ty: &Type, reexport_ctx: Option<&ReexportTypeContext>) -> String {
    match ty {
        Type::Void => "()".to_string(),
        Type::Bool => "bool".to_string(),
        Type::I32 => "i32".to_string(),
        Type::U32 => "u32".to_string(),
        Type::U16 => "u16".to_string(),
        Type::I16 => "i16".to_string(),
        Type::I64 => "i64".to_string(),
        Type::U64 => "u64".to_string(),
        Type::Long => "std::ffi::c_long".to_string(),
        Type::ULong => "std::ffi::c_ulong".to_string(),
        Type::Usize => "usize".to_string(),
        Type::F32 => "f32".to_string(),
        Type::F64 => "f64".to_string(),
        Type::CHAR16 => "u16".to_string(),
        Type::U8 => "u8".to_string(),
        Type::I8 => "i8".to_string(),
        Type::Class(name) => {
            if name == "char" {
                "std::ffi::c_char".to_string()
            } else if is_void_ptr_type_name(name) {
                "*mut std::ffi::c_void".to_string()
            } else if name == "void" {
                "std::ffi::c_void".to_string()
            } else if let Some(ffi_ty) = std_bitmask_ffi_type(name) {
                ffi_ty.to_rust_type_string()
            } else if let Some(ctx) = reexport_ctx {
                ctx.resolve_class(name)
            } else {
                format!("crate::ffi::{}", Type::ffi_safe_class_name(name))
            }
        }
        Type::Handle(name) => format!("crate::ffi::{}", type_mapping::handle_type_name(name)),
        Type::FixedArray(inner, size) => {
            format!("[{}; {}]", type_to_rust_string(inner, reexport_ctx), size)
        }
        Type::ConstRef(inner) => format!("&{}", type_to_rust_string(inner, reexport_ctx)),
        Type::MutRef(inner) => {
            format!("&mut {}", type_to_rust_string(inner, reexport_ctx))
        }
        Type::RValueRef(_) => "()".to_string(),
        Type::ConstPtr(inner) => {
            if matches!(inner.as_ref(), Type::Class(name) if name == "char") {
                "&str".to_string()
            } else {
                format!("*const {}", type_to_rust_string(inner, reexport_ctx))
            }
        }
        Type::MutPtr(inner) => format!("*mut {}", type_to_rust_string(inner, reexport_ctx)),
    }
}

/// Convert a return Type to Rust type string for re-export files
fn return_type_to_rust_string(ty: &Type, reexport_ctx: Option<&ReexportTypeContext>) -> String {
    match ty {
        Type::Class(name) if is_opaque_class_name(name) => {
            let inner = if let Some(ctx) = reexport_ctx {
                ctx.resolve_class(name)
            } else {
                format!("crate::ffi::{}", Type::ffi_safe_class_name(name))
            };
            format!("crate::OwnedPtr<{}>", inner)
        }
        Type::Handle(name) => {
            format!(
                "crate::OwnedPtr<crate::ffi::{}>",
                type_mapping::handle_type_name(name)
            )
        }
        Type::ConstPtr(inner) if matches!(inner.as_ref(), Type::Class(name) if name == "char") => {
            "std::string::String".to_string()
        }
        // Class pointer returns -> Option<&T> / Option<&mut T>
        Type::ConstPtr(inner) if matches!(inner.as_ref(), Type::Class(name) if !is_void_type_name(name)) => {
            if let T
```

```
fn emit_free_function_wrapper(
    output: &mut String,
    func: &super::bindings::FunctionBinding,
) {
    use std::fmt::Write;

    // Source attribution + doc comment
    let source_attr = format_source_attribution(
        &func.source_header,
        func.source_line,
        &format!("{}::{}", func.namespace, func.short_name),
    );
    writeln!(output, "/// {}", source_attr).unwrap();
    if let Some(ref comment) = func.doc_comment {
        emit_doc_comment(output, comment, "");
    }
    if func.unsafe_lifetime {
        output.push_str(super::bindings::format_lifetime_safety_doc_free_fn());
    }

    // Build parameter list using pre-computed re-export types.
    // For unsafe_lifetime free functions, add lifetime 'a to all reference params.
    let params: Vec<String> = func.params.iter()
        .map(|p| {
            if func.unsafe_lifetime {
                let ty = add_lifetime_to_ref_type(&p.rust_reexport_type);
                format!("{}: {}", p.rust_name, ty)
            } else {
                format!("{}: {}", p.rust_name, p.rust_reexport_type)
            }
        })
        .collect();

    // Build args with proper conversions: nullable ptr, class ptr, enum, CString, &mut enum
    let args: Vec<String> = func.params.iter()
        .map(|p| super::bindings::convert_arg(p))
        .collect();

    // Generate prelude for CString (&str) params and &mut enum params
    let prelude = super::bindings::cstr_prelude_params(&func.params, "    ");

    // Build return type string.
    // For unsafe_lifetime free functions, add lifetime 'a to reference return types.
    let return_type_str = func.return_type.as_ref()
        .map(|rt| {
            if func.unsafe_lifetime {
                format!(" -> {}", add_lifetime_to_ref_type(&rt.rust_reexport_type))
            } else {
                format!(" -> {}", rt.rust_reexport_type)
            }
        })
        .unwrap_or_default();

    // Build call expression args
    let args_str = args.join(", ");

    // Build body with proper conversions: enum returns, OwnedPtr wrapping, pointer-to-reference, class ptr returns
    let body = super::bindings::build_reexport_body(
        &func.cpp_wrapper_name,
        &args_str,
        func.return_type.as_ref(),
    );

    // Generate postamble for &mut enum writeback
    let postamble = super::bindings::mut_ref_enum_postamble_params(&func.params, "    ");
    let has_return = !return_type_str.is_empty();
    let body = super::bindings::wrap_body_with_postamble(&body, &postamble, has_return);

    let unsafe_kw = if func.is_unsafe { "unsafe " } else { "" };
    let lifetime_param = if func.unsafe_lifetime { "<'a>" } else { "" };
    writeln!(output, "pub {}fn {}{}({}){} {{", unsafe_kw, func.rust_ffi_name, lifetime_param, params.join(", "), return_type_str).unwrap();
    write!(output, "{}", prelude).unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "    {}", body).unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "}}").unwrap();
}
```

<summary>Reading lines 4514-4570 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs#L4514-L4571">bindings.rs</a></summary>

```
pub(super) fn build_reexport_body(ffi_fn_name: &str, args_str: &str, rt: Option<&ReturnTypeBinding>) -> String {
    let reexport_type = rt.map(|r| r.rust_reexport_type.as_str());
    let is_enum = rt.and_then(|r| r.enum_rust_type.as_deref());
    let needs_owned_ptr = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.needs_unique_ptr);
    let is_class_ptr_return = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.is_class_ptr_return);
    let is_mut_ref_enum_return = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.is_mut_ref_enum_return);

    // Build the FFI call expression
    let call = if args_str.is_empty() {
        format!("crate::ffi::{}()", ffi_fn_name)
    } else {
        format!("crate::ffi::{}({})", ffi_fn_name, args_str)
    };

    // Void return: call returns *const c_char (null = success)
    if rt.is_none() || reexport_type.is_none() {
        return format!("crate::check_void_result(unsafe {{ {} }})", call);
    }

    // check_result extracts .ret after checking .exc
    // "bare" form has no unsafe around the FFI call (for use inside an outer unsafe block)
    // "checked" form wraps the FFI call in unsafe (for standalone use)
    let checked_bare = format!("crate::check_result({})", call);
    let checked = format!("crate::check_result(unsafe {{ {} }})", call);

    // Cases that need a let binding (result used in conditional or cast)
    if is_mut_ref_enum_return {
        if let Some(enum_type) = is_enum {
            return format!("{{ let __val = {}; unsafe {{ &mut *(__val as *mut {}) }} }}", checked, enum_type);
        } else {
            return format!("unsafe {{ &mut *({}) }}", checked_bare);
        }
    }
    if is_class_ptr_return {
        if let Some(rty) = reexport_type {
            if rty.starts_with("Option<&mut ") {
                return format!("{{ let __val = {}; if __val.is_null() {{ None }} else {{ Some(unsafe {{ &mut *__val }}) }} }}", checked);
            }
        }
        return format!("{{ let __val = {}; if __val.is_null() {{ None }} else {{ Some(unsafe {{ &*__val }}) }} }}", checked);
    }
    if let Some(enum_type) = is_enum {
        return format!("{}::try_from({}).unwrap()", enum_type, checked);
    }
    if needs_owned_ptr {
        return format!("unsafe {{ crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw({}) }}", checked_bare);
    }
    if let Some(rty) = reexport_type {
        if rty == "std::string::String" {
            return format!("unsafe {{ std::ffi::CStr::from_ptr({}) }}.to_string_lossy().into_owned()", checked_bare);
        } else if rty.starts_with("&mut ") {
            return format!("unsafe {{ &mut *({}) }}", checked_bare);
        } else if rty.starts_with('&') {
            return format!("unsafe {{ &*({}) }}", checked_bare);
        }
    }
    // Simple passthrough (primitives, bool)
```

```
    }

    // 4. Generate lib.rs with module declarations
    let lib_rs = generate_lib_rs(&generated_modules, &extra_only_modules);
    let lib_rs_path = args.output.join("lib.rs");
    std::fs::write(&lib_rs_path, &lib_rs)?;
    generated_rs_files.push(lib_rs_path.clone());
    println!("  Wrote: {}", lib_rs_path.display());

    // Format generated Rust files
    if !generated_rs_files.is_empty() {
        println!("\nFormatting generated Rust code with rustfmt...");
        let status = Command::new("rustfmt")
            .arg("+nightly")
            .args(&generated_rs_files)
            .status();

        match status {
            Ok(s) if s.success() => println!("  Formatting complete."),
            Ok(s) => eprintln!("  Warning: rustfmt exited with status: {}", s),
            Err(e) => eprintln!("  Warning: Failed to run rustfmt: {}", e),
        }

        // No post-processing needed - string-based generation emits correct output directly
    }

    println!("\nCode generation complete!");
    println!("  {} modules generated", generated_modules.len());

    Ok(())
}

/// Generate lib.rs with module declarations
fn generate_lib_rs(modules: &[&module_graph::Module], extra_modules: &[(String, String)]) -> String {
    let mut output = String::new();
    output.push_str("// Generated OCCT bindings\n");
    output.push_str("// Nested C++ types use Parent_Child naming, which is intentional\n");
    output.push_str("#![allow(non_camel_case_types)]\n\n");

    output.push_str("// Core FFI module with all types (pub(crate) to prevent direct access, use module re-exports instead)\n");
    output.push_str("pub(crate) mod ffi;\n\n");
    output.push_str("// Per-module re-exports\n");

    // Collect all module rust names and sort for deterministic output
    let mut all_rust_names: Vec<&str> = modules.iter().map(|m| m.rust_name.as_str()).collect();
    for (_, rust_name) in extra_modules {
        all_rust_names.push(rust_name);
    }
    all_rust_names.sort();
    all_rust_names.dedup();

    for rust_name in all_rust_names {
        output.push_str(&format!("pub mod {};\n", rust_name));
    }

    output
}
```

```
            let module_collections: Vec<_> = all_collections
                .iter()
                .filter(|c| c.module == rust_name)
                .collect();
            let empty_fn_bindings: Vec<&codegen::bindings::FunctionBinding> = Vec::new();
            let module_fn_bindings = fn_bindings_by_module
                .get(&rust_name)
                .unwrap_or(&empty_fn_bindings);
            let empty_skipped_fns: Vec<&codegen::bindings::SkippedSymbol> = Vec::new();
            let module_skipped_fns = skipped_fns_by_module
                .get(&rust_name)
                .unwrap_or(&empty_skipped_fns);
            let reexport_code = codegen::rust::generate_module_reexports(
                module_name,
                &rust_name,
                &[],
                &module_collections,
                symbol_table,
                &[],
                module_fn_bindings,
                module_skipped_fns,
                types,
            );
            let module_path = args.output.join(format!("{}.rs", rust_name));
            let mut reexport_code = reexport_code;
            if let Some(include) = manual_include_directive(&args.output, &rust_name) {
                reexport_code.push_str(&include);
            }
            std::fs::write(&module_path, &reexport_code)?;
            generated_rs_files.push(module_path.clone());
            extra_only_modules.push((module_name.clone(), rust_name.clone()));
            println!("  Wrote: {} (extra types only, {} types)", module_path.display(), types.len());
        }
    }

    // Generate module files for function-only modules (utility classes converted
    // to free functions that left no classes behind in the module)
    let extra_type_modules: HashSet<String> = extra_only_modules.iter().map(|(_, r)| r.clone()).collect();
    let _empty_fn_bindings_vec: Vec<&codegen::bindings::FunctionBinding> = Vec::new();
    for (rust_module, fn_bindings) in &fn_bindings_by_module {
        if graph_rust_names.contains(rust_module) || extra_type_modules.contains(rust_module) {
            continue;
        }
        if fn_bindings.is_empty() {
            continue;
        }
        // Derive the C++ module name from the namespace of the first function
        let cpp_name = fn_bindings[0].namespace.clone();
        let empty_skipped_fns: Vec<&codegen::bindings::SkippedSymbol> = Vec::new();
        let module_skipped_fns = skipped_fns_by_module
            .get(rust_module)
            .unwrap_or(&empty_skipped_fns);
        let reexport_code = codegen::rust::generate_module_reexports(
            &cpp_name,
            rust_module,
            &[],
            &[],
            symbol_table,
            &[],
            fn_bindings,
            module_skipped_fns,
            &[],
        );
        let module_path = args.output.join(format!("{}.rs", rust_module));
        let mut reexport_code = reexport_code;
        if let Some(include) = manual_include_directive(&args.output, rust_module) {
            reexport_code.push_str(&include);
        }
        std::fs::write(&module_path, &reexport_code)?;
        generated_rs_files.push(module_path.clone());
        extra_only_modules.push((cpp_name.clone(), rust_module.clone()));
        println!("  Wrote: {} (function-only module, {} functions)", module_path.display(), fn_bindings.len());
    }

    // 4. Generate lib.rs with module declarations
    let lib_rs = generate_lib_rs(&generated_modules, &extra_only_modules);
    let lib_rs_path = args.output.join("lib.rs");
    std::fs::write(&lib_rs_path, &lib_rs)?;
```
</details>

> 3. Line 1505 - for function-only modules

> 2. Add it as a parameter to:

>    - `emit_reexport_class()` 
>    - `build_reexport_body()`
>    - `emit_free_function_wrapper()`
>    - `emit_collection_impl()`

> 3. In all format strings, replace `"crate::ffi"` with the appropriate field from FfiPaths

> 
> 5. In generate_ffi_split, emit proper standalone modules

> 6. In generate_lib_rs, add ffi_extern module declarations
> 
> I'm defining the FfiPaths struct with three fields: types for struct definitions, fns for class extern functions, and coll_fns for collection functions. For non-split mode all three point to "crate::ffi", but in split mode they diverge to their respective modules like "crate::ffi_types" and "crate::ffi_extern_TKMath".
>

I can add `ffi_type_module` to `ReexportTypeContext` and use it in the fallback at the resolve_class and Handle reference sites. The tricky part is that `type_to_rust_string` is sometimes called without a reexport context, so I need to figure out where those calls happen and whether they need updating too.

> After checking the grep results, it looks like `type_to_rust_string` is always called with a context in the reexport code path, so I can safely add `ffi_type_module` to the context and use it for both the Class fallback and Handle type resolution. For the None case, I'll keep `"crate::ffi"` as the default. This keeps the changes localized to the type resolution functions and the ReexportTypeContext construction.
> 
> Now I need to thread `ffi_fn_module` through several functions: `build_reexport_body`, `emit_reexport_class`, `emit_free_function_wrapper`, `emit_collection_impl`, and `generate_module_reexports`. The file reorganization in `generate_ffi_split` and `generate_lib_rs` will also need adjustments to handle the new module structure. This is a large refactoring, so I'll tackle it in phases: first the struct and type changes, then the function call changes, then the file organization updates. I'm realizing there are roughly 80+ individual string edits scattered across multiple functions and files. Rather than making changes piecemeal, I should batch them by file using hashline_edit to be more efficient. I'll start by grouping the changes in bindings.rs—the ReexportTypeContext, resolve_class, and the type/return type conversion functions are all clustered together, so I can tackle those in one or two batched calls, then move through rust.rs and main.rs systematically.

> 💭 OK so `ReexportTypeContext` is created at two places in bindings.rs during binding computation. It's used to compute the pre-stored `rust_reexport_type` strings for params and return types. These strings contain `crate::ffi::` references for types.
> 
> The key insight: the `rust_reexport_type` is computed once and stored. If I change the ReexportTypeContext to use `"crate::ffi_types"` instead of `"crate::ffi"`, then ALL pre-computed type strings will use the new module name. This works because:
> - In split mode, ALL types are in ffi_types.rs (always the same module)
> 
> 
> - In non-split mode, ALL types are in ffi.rs (always the same module)
> 
> So I need to pass `ffi_type_module: &str` to the functions that create ReexportTypeContext and thread it from main.rs. Since bindings are computed before per-module files are generated, the ffi_type_module choice affects all bindings uniformly, which is exactly what I want. My approach is to add `ffi_type_module` to ReexportTypeContext and use it in `resolve_class()`.
> 
> Now I'm thinking through how to thread this parameter back through the construction sites to main.rs. For function module paths that vary per toolkit, those only matter during emission, so they get passed directly to the emit functions. Given the scope of changes needed, I'm considering whether a re-export shim approach might be simpler—renaming ffi.rs to ffi_types.rs for struct definitions, making ffi_extern_*.rs standalone modules that import from ffi_types, then creating a new ffi.rs that re-exports everything. This keeps per-module files using `crate::ffi::` unchanged while still achieving separate compilation and avoiding include!() macros, though it does add a re-export layer. I'm realizing the re-export approach keeps all the dependencies in one place, which doesn't actually help with incremental compilation the way the user wants. They're right that direct per-module references would reduce the dependency chain for each file. The tradeoff is those 80+ format string changes, but I could automate this with a two-pass generator approach: first emit the per-module files with placeholder references, then systematically replace them with the correct module paths.
> 
> Actually, a cleaner solution is post-processing the generated code with regex to distinguish function calls from type references—function calls have `(` immediately after the identifier, while type references don't. I can replace `crate::ffi::name(` with the appropriate extern module and leave remaining `crate::ffi::` references to become `crate::ffi_types::`. This keeps the generator logic simple while achieving the per-module split the user wants. I'm realizing that collection CppDeletable implementations are already in the per-module files, so if I distribute collection functions to their toolkit extern blocks, the destructors would naturally align with them. My plan is to modify `generate_ffi_split` to place collection functions directly into their respective toolkit blocks instead of a separate collections file, then handle nested type destructors the same way, and finally post-process the per-module files with a regex to wire everything together.
> 
> The regex approach is simpler than modifying 80+ format strings across the codebase—I'd just replace `crate::ffi::name(` with `crate::ffi_extern_{toolkit}::name(` for function calls and leave remaining `crate::ffi::` references as type paths pointing to `crate::ffi_types::`. I'm checking edge cases like nested function calls, type casts, and use statements to make sure the regex handles them correctly without breaking anything.

**Key insight**: Instead of modifying 80+ format strings in the generator, I'll use a **post-processing** approach:

1. Generate per-module .rs files exactly as before (with `crate::ffi::`)
2. Post-process each module file: replace `crate::ffi::X(` → `crate::ffi_extern_{toolkit}::X(` (function calls) and remaining `crate::ffi::` → `crate::ffi_types::` (type refs)
3. Also distribute collection functions into their toolkit extern blocks (no separate collections file)
4. Rename ffi.rs → ffi_types.rs, remove include!(), each extern file gets `use crate::ffi_types::*;`
5. Update lib.rs generation

```
/// Generate per-toolkit extern "C" block files and a facade ffi.rs for split mode.
///
/// Returns (ffi_facade_code, per_toolkit_extern_files) where:
/// - ffi_facade_code is the ffi.rs content with struct defs + include!() macros
/// - per_toolkit_extern_files is a Vec of (toolkit_name, extern_block_code)
pub fn generate_ffi_split(
    all_classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    all_functions: &[&crate::model::ParsedFunction],
    all_headers: &[String],
    collections: &[super::collections::CollectionInfo],
    symbol_table: &crate::resolver::SymbolTable,
    all_bindings: &[super::bindings::ClassBindings],
    function_bindings: &[super::bindings::FunctionBinding],
    handle_able_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    extra_typedef_names: &HashSet<String>,
    non_allocatable_classes: &HashSet<String>,
    module_to_toolkit: &std::collections::HashMap<String, String>,
) -> (String, Vec<NestedTypeInfo>, Vec<(String, String)>) {
    // First generate the monolithic output to extract struct definitions
    let (monolithic, nested_types) = generate_ffi(
        all_classes, all_functions, all_headers, collections,
        symbol_table, all_bindings, function_bindings,
        handle_able_classes, extra_typedef_names, non_allocatable_classes,
    );

    // Split the monolithic output at the last extern "C" block
    // The struct defs are before it, the methods are inside it
    // Find the main extern "C" { block (the one containing class methods)
    // It's the last "extern \"C\" {" in the file
    let extern_pos = monolithic.rfind("\nextern \"C\" {").map(|p| p + 1);
    let (struct_defs, _extern_block) = if let Some(pos) = extern_pos {
        (&monolithic[..pos], &monolithic[pos..])
    } else {
        (monolithic.as_str(), "")
    };

    let collection_type_names: HashSet<String> = collections.iter()
        .map(|c| c.typedef_name.clone())
        .collect();

    // Helper to extract C++ module from name prefix
    let cpp_module = |name: &str| -> String {
        name.split('_').next().unwrap_or("").to_string()
    };

    // Group bindings by toolkit
    let mut tk_bindings: std::collections::HashMap<String, Vec<&super::bindings::ClassBindings>> =
        std::collections::HashMap::new();
    for b in all_bindings {
        if collection_type_names.contains(&b.cpp_name) {
            continue;
        }
        let module = cpp_module(&b.cpp_name);
        let toolkit = module_to_toolkit.get(&module)
            .cloned()
            .unwrap_or_else(|| "misc".to_string());
        tk_bindings.entry(toolkit).or_default().push(b);
    }

    // Group function bindings by toolkit
    let mut tk_functions: std::collections::HashMap<String, Vec<&super::bindings::FunctionBinding>> =
        std::collections::HashMap::new();
    for fb in function_bindings {
        let module = &fb.namespace;
        let toolkit = module_to_toolkit.get(module)
            .cloned()
            .unwrap_or_else(|| "misc".to_string());
        tk_functions.entry(toolkit).or_default().push(fb);
    }

    // Collect all toolkit names
    let mut all_toolkits: std::collections::HashSet<String> = std::collections::HashSet::new();
    all_toolkits.extend(tk_bindings.keys().cloned());
    all_toolkits.extend(tk_functions.keys().cloned());
    let mut sorted_toolkits: Vec<_> = all_toolkits.into_iter().collect();
    sorted_toolkits.sort();

    // Generate per-toolkit extern block files
    let mut toolkit_files: Vec<(String, String)> = Vec::new();
    for toolkit in &sorted_toolkits {
        let mut out = String::new();
        writeln!(out, "// Generated by opencascade-binding-generator").unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "// FFI extern block for toolkit: {}", toolkit).unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "// Do not edit this file directly.").unwrap();
        writeln!(out).unwrap();
        writeln!(out, "extern \"C\" {{").unwrap();

        // Class methods
        if let Some(bindings) = tk_bindings.get(toolkit.as_str()) {
            for b in bindings {
        
... (truncated, 5904 chars)
```

Now let me write the new implementation. I'll rewrite `generate_ffi_split` to:
1. Group collections by toolkit alongside class bindings and functions
2. Group nested type destructors by toolkit 
3. Produce standalone `.rs` module files with `use crate::ffi_types::*;`

```
    }

    // Include collection extern block
    if let Some(coll_code) = coll_extern {
        writeln!(facade, "include!(\"ffi_extern_collections.rs\");").unwrap();
        toolkit_files.push(("__collections".to_string(), coll_code));
    }

    // CppDeletable impls for nested types (after all extern blocks)
    if !nested_types.is_empty() {
        writeln!(facade).unwrap();
        writeln!(facade, "// CppDeletable impls for nested and typedef types").unwrap();
        for nt in &nested_types {
            writeln!(facade, "unsafe impl crate::CppDeletable for {} {{", nt.ffi_name).unwrap();
            writeln!(facade, "    unsafe fn cpp_delete(ptr: *mut Self) {{").unwrap();
            writeln!(facade, "        {}_destructor(ptr);", nt.ffi_name).unwrap();
            writeln!(facade, "    }}").unwrap();
            writeln!(facade, "}}").unwrap();
        }
    }

    (facade, nested_types, toolkit_files)
}
```

It takes `&[CollectionInfo]` but I'm passing `&[&CollectionInfo]`. I need to either change the signature or collect the references. Let me make it generic like I did for `generate_functions_from_bindings`:

```
pub fn generate_rust_ffi_collections(collections: &[CollectionInfo]) -> (String, String) {
    if collections.is_empty() {
        return (String::new(), String::new());
    }
    
    // Generate opaque struct declarations (outside extern "C" block)
    let mut type_decls = String::new();
    type_decls.push_str("// ========================\n");
    type_decls.push_str("// Collection types (opaque)\n");
    type_decls.push_str("// ========================\n\n");
    for info in collections {
        writeln!(type_decls, "#[repr(C)]").unwrap();
        writeln!(type_decls, "pub struct {} {{ _opaque: [u8; 0] }}", info.typedef_name).unwrap();
        // Iterator type
        let iter_name = format!("{}Iterator", info.short_name);
        writeln!(type_decls, "#[repr(C)]").unwrap();
        writeln!(type_decls, "pub struct {} {{ _opaque: [u8; 0] }}", iter_name).unwrap();
    }
    type_decls.push('\n');
    
    let mut ffi_decls = String::new();
    
    ffi_decls.push_str("    // ========================\n");
    ffi_decls.push_str("    // Collection type wrappers\n");
    ffi_decls.push_str("    // ========================\n\n");
    
    for info in collections {
        ffi_decls.push_str(&generate_rust_ffi_collection(info));
    }
    
    (type_decls, ffi_decls)
}

```

```
    let nested_types = if split_config.ffi_split.as_deref() == Some("toolkit") {
        println!("Generating split ffi.rs (per-toolkit extern blocks)...");
        let (ffi_code, nested_types, toolkit_files) = codegen::rust::generate_ffi_split(

        generated_rs_files.push(ffi_path.clone());
        println!("  Wrote: {} (facade)", ffi_path.display());

        // Write per-toolkit extern block files
        for (toolkit_name, code) in &toolkit_files {
            let filename = if toolkit_name.starts_with("__") {
                format!("ffi_extern_{}.rs", &toolkit_name[2..])
            } else {
                format!("ffi_extern_{}.rs", toolkit_name)
            };
            let path = args.output.join(&filename);
            std::fs::write(&path, code)?;
            generated_rs_files.push(path.clone());
            println!("  Wrote: {}", path.display());
        }
        println!("  {} toolkit extern files", toolkit_files.len());
        nested_types
    } else {
```
